text:
I am filing this complaint because Wells Fargo failed to properly investigate and resolve fraudulent electronic fund transfers totaling {$4000.00} from my checking account and denied my claim without providing an adequate explanation, despite the disputed transactions appearing on my account statement. 
On XX/XX/XXXX, I was selling a chair on XXXX XXXX when I was contacted by an individual posing as a buyer. During the transaction, I was deceived by a scammer impersonating XXXX XXXX support and was fraudulently induced into initiating transfers from my Wells Fargo account in the amounts of {$2000.00}, {$1000.00}, and {$1000.00}, totaling {$4000.00}. 
The scammer falsely represented that these transfers were part of a secure verification process required to receive payment for the item I was selling and assured me that the funds were safe and would be returned. I relied on these material misrepresentations when initiating the transfers. 
As soon as I realized I had been defrauded, I immediately contacted Wells Fargo to report the fraudulent transfers and requested an investigation. I also provided evidence supporting my claim, including account statements showing the disputed transactions, screenshots, and recorded evidence demonstrating that the transfers were induced by false representations. 
Despite this, Wells Fargo denied my claim and stated that it did not see the {$4000.00} in withdrawals, even though the disputed transfers are clearly reflected on my checking account statement. Wells Fargo did not explain how it reviewed my claim, what transactions were reviewed, why the statement-documented transfers were not recognized, or why the evidence I submitted was disregarded. 
Wells Fargos response indicates that it failed to conduct a reasonable investigation of the disputed transfers and failed to provide the basis for its denial. Although I physically initiated the transfers, my authorization was obtained through fraudulent impersonation and material misrepresentation. Wells Fargo failed to meaningfully review the evidence that these transfers were fraudulently induced. 
I am requesting that Wells Fargo : Reopen and conduct a full investigation into the XXXX disputed transfers totaling {$4000.00} Provide the records and findings relied upon in denying my claim Reimburse the {$4000.00} removed from my account as a result of this fraud I have also filed reports with the FTC and local law enforcement and am prepared to provide all supporting documentation, including statements, screenshots, and evidence of the scam.

text:
I am filing a complaint against Truist regarding what I believe to be an improper extended hold placed on a check deposit in violation of Regulation CC. 

On Sunday, XX/XX/XXXX, I deposited a {$400.00} mobile check from a XXXX XXXX refund. When I called the next day to ask about the 7 day hold, I was informed that the funds would be available within 1-2 days. However, after waiting the check was still placed on a 7-day hold without proper justification. 

I was told the hold was due to repeated overdrafts. This is inaccurate. My account has only been overdrawn for 3 days, which does not meet the Regulation CC threshold of 6 or more days required to classify an account as repeatedly overdrawn. I was also told that my account was considered new because it is less than two years old. This is inconsistent with Regulation CC, which defines a new account as one open for less than 30 days.

I requested that the check be verified, including confirmation of the routing number, and asked that the funds be released based on that verification. The bank manager refused both to verify the check and to release the funds, without providing a valid explanation that aligns with Regulation CC guidelines. 

Based on these facts, I believe Truist has improperly applied an extended hold and failed to follow federal funds availability requirements. I am requesting that this matter be investigated, that the bank provide a valid justification for the hold, and that appropriate corrective action be taken.
label: account_management

text:
I am filing a complaint against Bank of America for improperly denying my fraud claim and failing to comply with federal law. 

On XX/XX/2026, I disputed unauthorized electronic transactions totaling {$1200.00}. I did not authorize these transactions, did not provide consent, and did not receive any goods or services. 

Bank of America denied my claim based on the assertion that the transactions were authorized because they were conducted using my device and/or XXXX XXXX. This reasoning is legally insufficient.

Under the Electronic Fund Transfer Act ( 15 U.S.C. 1693 ) and Regulation E ( 12 CFR 1005 ), an electronic fund transfer is considered authorized only if it is initiated with the consumers actual knowledge and express consent. The use of a device, IP address, passcode, or biometric authentication ( such as Face ID ) does NOT, by itself, establish authorization.

Unauthorized use of a device even if it appears linked to the consumer does not meet the legal standard for authorization.

Bank of America has failed to demonstrate that I provided valid consent for these transactions. They have also failed to provide sufficient evidence to support their conclusion, despite my request for documentation such as IP logs, device identification data, and merchant verification.

Under 12 CFR 1005.11, the bank is required to conduct a reasonable and good-faith investigation. Denying my claim based solely on internal technical indicators, without proving actual authorization, does not satisfy this legal obligation.

I am requesting the following : * Immediate reopening and escalation of my claim * Reimbursement of the full disputed amount of {$1200.00} * Full disclosure of all evidence used in the investigation, including technical and merchant records If this matter is not resolved promptly, I will pursue further action through regulatory enforcement channels and legal remedies.
label: account_management
source_sub_issue: Problem using a debit or ATM card

text:
Dear CFPB, I am writing to update my existing complaint ( # XXXX ) against Citibank , N.A . Since my initial filing, Citibank has now denied my dispute twice including on formal appeal and continues to misapply federal law under Regulation E. I respectfully request that the CFPB escalate its review of this matter. 

BACKGROUND SUMMARY I paid TXN Gourmet Catering {$23000.00} via XXXX XXXX ( Citi checking account ending in XXXX ) in three installments between XXXX and XX/XX/XXXX for catering services at my son 's engagement party on XX/XX/XXXX. The merchant failed to appear and provide any services. On XX/XX/XXXX, XXXX XXXX XXXX confirmed via email that they had issued a full refund of {$23000.00}. That refund never arrived. After the merchant became completely unreachable on or around XX/XX/XXXX, I contacted Citibank to dispute the transactions on XX/XX/XXXX. 

CITIBANK 'S FIRST DENIAL XX/XX/XXXX Citibank 's Executive Response Unit denied my dispute, claiming I failed to report the transactions in a timely manner. Additionally, their letter only acknowledged two of the three disputed transactions, leaving the {$7800.00} claim ( XXXX ) unaddressed a clear procedural error on Citibank 's part. 

CITIBANK 'S SECOND DENIAL ( APPEAL ) XX/XX/XXXX Following my formal rebuttal letter dated XX/XX/XXXX, which cited Regulation E and demanded a proper reinvestigation, Citibank issued a second denial on XX/XX/XXXX. They again cited timeliness, stating the disputes were " reported outside of the required timeframe. '' This denial ignores the legal basis I provided and fails to engage with the substance of my appeal. 

WHY CITIBANK 'S POSITION VIOLATES REGULATION E Citibank 's timeliness argument is legally incorrect for the following reasons : The 60-day Regulation E clock runs from the date the error appears on the periodic statement. However, for services not yet rendered at the time of payment, the consumer can not reasonably discover the problem until the service date passes. 
The service date was XX/XX/XXXX. There was no dispute to file prior to that date, as the services had not yet failed to be delivered. 
On XX/XX/XXXX, the merchant issued a written promise of a full refund. I reasonably relied on this promise and waited for the refund to post, as any prudent consumer would. 
When the merchant went silent after XX/XX/XXXX, I immediately contacted Citibank on XX/XX/XXXX within 60 days of discovering that the promised refund would not be issued. This is clearly within the Regulation XXXX window measured from the date of reasonable discovery. 


I have attached the following supporting documents to this update : Citibank denial letter dated XX/XX/XXXX Citibank appeal denial letter dated XX/XX/XXXX My formal rebuttal letter dated XX/XX/XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX email confirming {$23000.00} refund ( XX/XX/XXXX ) Full email chain showing repeated follow-ups before contacting Citibank I XXXX the CFPB 's XXXX to this matter and trust that this update will prompt the appropriate escalation. 

Respectfully submitted, XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, NY XXXX ( XXXX ) XXXX CFPB Case # XXXX

text:
This complaint concerns J.P. Morgan/Chases refusal or failure to provide a consistent online method for customers to add or update beneficiaries for certain account types ( checking, savings ), while providing digital beneficiary-management functionality for other account categories ( XXXX XXXX XXXX ). Chases own beneficiary FAQ states that a beneficiary can be added to a Chase account by visiting a branch, while its investment materials separately state that customers with J.P. Morgan XXXX XXXX investment accounts can designate or update beneficiaries through the Chase Mobile app or on chase.com. 

The result is a fragmented and burdensome process in which customers may be required to appear in person for some accounts, even though the institution already maintains secure digital channels for beneficiary updates for other products. This discrepancy creates unnecessary friction in basic account administration and undermines efficient estate planning for deposit-account holders and similarly situated consumers. 

These interactions imposed avoidable time, travel, scheduling, and administrative burdens for a task that should be manageable through authenticated digital banking channels. Chases own public materials show that online beneficiary management is available for certain investment accounts, which demonstrates that the bank already has the operational capability to support remote beneficiary maintenance in at least part of its product ecosystem. 

The inability to update beneficiaries online is not a minor convenience issue. Beneficiary designations help assets pass directly to designated individuals and can reduce the probability of a drawn-out probate process. When a customer can not promptly complete or revise those designations, estate planning may be delayed, family expectations may remain unclear, and account administration at death may become more complicated than necessary.

This issue is especially serious when customers are XXXX, XXXX, traveling, working irregular hours, managing family emergencies, or otherwise unable to appear at a branch easily. Requiring an in-person visit for a routine beneficiary update creates a disproportionate burden when comparable financial servicing functions are commonly handled through secure online and mobile channels, and when Chase itself already permits digital beneficiary updates for J.P. Morgan XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. 

J.P. Morgan/Chase appears to maintain different servicing standards depending on account type : branch-based beneficiary changes for at least some Chase accounts, but digital self-service for certain investment accounts. If the institution can authenticate customers and process beneficiary changes online for one class of accounts, it should be able to explain clearly why similarly sensitive changes for ordinary consumer deposit accounts require branch attendance rather than secure digital workflows, identity verification, and electronic acknowledgment. 

In addition, J.P. Morgan asset-management materials indicate that beneficiary designations for some non-qualified and IRA accounts are handled through paper forms submitted by mail, further illustrating that the customer experience varies significantly across account types and may depend on legacy processing channels rather than modern digital convenience. From a consumer-protection perspective, such inconsistency can cause confusion, delay, and needless effort in an area closely tied to end-of-life planning and family financial security.

I attempted to resolve this by asking this via their secured channel and I was told that I need to visit a branch. 

My son on in separate account visited the branch for updating beneficiary, he was made to sign a docusign application for beneficiary update but never got its copy in his email. He was not even given a print out of his update. 

So visiting the branch and updating beneficiary is not helping. Investigation is required if JPMC is intentionally doing this to have no record with account holder so they can keep account holders money for a longer period of time during and make the family go through complex litigation. If so, how many such cases are impacted? 

A XXXX XXXX on this subject ( attached ) got over XXXX views and I will be writing to SEC separately on this to have formal investigation on the intent behind this given that other banks such as XXXX  XXXX and XXXX XXXX allow the beneficiaries to be updated online
label: account_management

text:
You'll need a scrubbed version since they prohibit personal identifiers in the narrative. Here 's a clean paste, with name and account numbers stripped : On XX/XX/year>, Citibank returned a {$1700.00} ACH debit from my Citibank XXXX XXXX account that had presented against sufficient funds, while honoring a separate, larger ACH debit that posted hours later and actually overdrew the account. Citibank labeled the return as an insufficient funds event. It was not. It was a discretionary selection by Citibank to return the smaller, covered item rather than the larger, overdrawing one. The return is the type of high to low transaction handling that has been the subject of substantial consumer protection litigation and regulatory action against other large institutions.

During my attempts to resolve the issue with Citibank by chat on XX/XX/year>, the escalation representative misrepresented the post-transaction balance, deflected with generic ACH definitions, and ultimately provided timestamps that confirmed the XXXX debit presented almost seven hours before the larger debit that overdrew the account. Citibank has nonetheless declined to reverse the return. 

FACTS As of XX/XX/year>, the available balance in the XXXX XXXX account was {$5600.00}. Citibank 's escalation representative confirmed this balance during the XX/XX/XXXX chat. 
Two ACH debits presented against the account on XX/XX/year> : a XXXX Bank personal loan payment of {$1700.00}, presented at XXXX XXXX, and a XXXX XXXX XXXX ACH debit of {$4000.00}, presented at XXXX XXXX. Both presentment timestamps were provided in writing by Citibank 's escalation representative. 

The XXXX item, standing alone, presented against an available balance of {$5600.00}. After the XXXX debit, the available balance was {$3900.00}. That figure also appears on Citibank 's own statement of account. The XXXX XXXX XXXX debit then presented almost seven hours later, against the post-XXXX  balance of {$3900.00}, and exceeded it by {$150.00}. 

In other words, the XXXX item had coverage when it presented. The XXXX item did not. 
On XX/XX/year>, Citibank returned the XXXX debit, posting it as " Returned Insufficient Funds, ACH Txn '' for {$1700.00}. Citibank honored the larger XXXX XXXX XXXX debit that overdrew the account. Citibank selected the smaller item that had coverage when it presented and returned it, while paying the larger item that did not have coverage when it presented. 

At XXXX XXXX  on XX/XX/year>, I transferred {$160.00} from my linked Citibank Checking account into the Savings Plus account, eliminating the negative balance the XXXX  debit had created. The XXXX item itself had not required any additional funds, having presented against sufficient funds the prior day. The transfer is noted only to confirm that any negative balance on the account was brief and was self-cured by me from another Citibank account . 

THE CHAT WITH CITIBANK On XX/XX/year>, I contacted Citibank by chat. The chat first routed me through an automated assistant offering options that did not describe my issue. After the system did not produce a live agent, I had to type a direct demand before it would route me to a person. 

The first representative provided a generic explanation of how overdrafts work, then confirmed he could see in the account details that there had been sufficient funds for the XXXX item, but offered no resolution. When I requested escalation, he initially proposed only to forward my comments as feedback. When I insisted, he transferred the chat. 

The escalation representative made the following statements, all preserved in the chat transcript. 

First, he represented that the post-XXXX  balance was negative {$150.00}, citing " our computerized records. '' That figure is the end of day balance after both XX/XX/XXXX debits had posted, not the balance after the XXXX item alone. The actual post-XXXX balance was {$3900.00}, as reflected on Citibank 's own statement and as the representative himself later confirmed in a transaction summary. 

Second, when I identified the error and asked why Citibank had selected the smaller, covered item for return rather than the larger overdrawing item, he responded with a paragraph defining ACH transactions and the statement that " Citi does not control the time of debiting the transaction. '' That statement does not address the discretionary return decision, which is made by Citibank, not by the ACH network.

Third, he provided a transaction summary listing the XXXX XXXX XXXX debit as " Debit 1 '' and the XXXX debit as " Debit 2. '' Fourth, he then provided the presentment timestamps, which showed the XXXX debit presenting at XXXX XXXX  on XX/XX/XXXX and the XXXX XXXX XXXX debit presenting at XXXX XXXX  on XX/XX/XXXX. His own message therefore contradicts itself : he labeled the XXXX item as the second debit while simultaneously timestamping it as having presented almost seven hours before the item he labeled the first debit.

Fifth, he offered to issue a " Transactions details letter confirming the time of ACH debits. '' I requested that letter.

Sixth, after I noted that his own timestamps proved the XXXX item had presented first and had coverage, he attempted to walk back the timestamps, asserted that order of debit is " totally dependent on the ACH network, '' and re-cited the end of day available balance of negative {$150.00} as the operative figure.

At no point did Citibank substantively address the central question : why Citibank chose to return the smaller item that had coverage at presentment and pay the larger item that did not.

CONCERNS Citibank 's conduct raises concerns under the prohibition on unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices in three respects. First, mischaracterization of the return, since the bank labeled a discretionary return as an insufficient funds return when the item had presented against more than three times the available funds it required. Second, discretionary selection of the smaller covered item over the larger overdrawing item, which is the inverse of consumer protective transaction handling and tracks the high to low patterns that have produced substantial litigation and regulatory action against other large institutions. Third, misrepresentation of the operative balance figure during the complaint process, repeated even after I had identified the error and even after the bank 's own timestamps proved my position.

DOWNSTREAM HARM The returned payment is a personal loan installment. The lender furnishes loan payment status to the major consumer credit bureaus. A 30 day late, which can be triggered by Citibank 's return, would result in adverse credit reporting and increased interest exposure beyond the late fee itself. The harm is concrete, foreseeable, and entirely a function of Citibank 's discretionary return decision.
label: account_management
source_sub_issue: Funds not handled or disbursed as instructed

text:
I am submitting this complaint regarding Bank of America and XXXX in connection with my XXXX XXXX XXXX and repeated failures to provide meaningful assistance, records, escalation, and access to funds necessary for my care and survival. 

I am a XXXX beneficiary of a XXXX XXXX XXXX that was established for my medical care, housing, disability support, and survival needs. Since approximately XX/XX/2025, I have repeatedly attempted to obtain help regarding access to trust funds, account records, trust administration, address updates, account management, and concerns involving unauthorized withdrawals and possible financial exploitation. 

The trust reportedly originally contained approximately {$180000.00}. By XX/XX/2025 the balance had reportedly decreased to approximately {$98000.00}, and the last statement I received reflected approximately {$44000.00} as of XX/XX/2025. I did not receive that statement until months later. Approximately {$21000.00} remains unaccounted for based on the records available to me. 

I repeatedly contacted Bank of America, XXXX, trust-related representatives, financial advisors, and associated parties requesting : * updated account statements and records, * a full accounting of transactions, * explanation of withdrawals and transfers, * help updating my address and communication information, * assistance accessing funds, * replacement or reassignment of financial advisor/trust contacts, * escalation regarding suspected financial exploitation and unauthorized withdrawals, * and guidance regarding my XXXX XXXX XXXX administration.

Despite repeated requests, I did not receive meaningful assistance or a complete accounting. In at least one instance, I received what appeared to be a blank PDF in response to an escalation request rather than the requested records or information.

During this same period, I was medically vulnerable, experiencing housing instability, and attempting to obtain medical care and survival resources while lacking meaningful access to the trust funds created for my protection and care. The lack of response and failure to escalate these concerns caused severe hardship and interfered with my ability to obtain housing, transportation, communication access, medical support, and basic necessities.

I am requesting investigation into : * failure to provide records and accountings, * failure to meaningfully respond to beneficiary concerns, * failure to escalate suspected financial exploitation concerns involving a disabled/dependent adult, * failure to assist with account management and trust access, * and failure to provide clear information regarding transactions, withdrawals, and responsible parties connected to the trust.
label: account_management

text:
XX/XX/XXXX Consumer Financial Protection Bureau XXXXXXXX XXXX XXXX Washington , DC XXXX ( Submission contemplated via consumerfinance.gov/complaint with this letter and the enclosures attached as supporting documents. ) Re : Complaint Against U.S. Bank National Association Inadequate Investigation, Inadequate Customer Communication, and Failure to Reimburse {$53000.00} in Unauthorized ACH Transactions ( Account ending in XXXX ) Dear Consumer Financial Protection Bureau : XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX ( formerly XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX ) ( XXXX ), an Arkansas business, submits this complaint against U.S. Bank National Association ( U.S. Bank ). XXXX account is a commercial deposit account, and XXXX acknowledges that some of the consumer-protection statutes and regulations the Bureau ordinarily enforces including Regulation E do not apply to commercial accounts. XXXX nonetheless brings this complaint to the Bureau because the conduct described below an inadequate fraud investigation, an unsubstantiated denial of reimbursement, a closing communication that contained no findings, and a formal response that mischaracterizes the customers contact implicates U.S. Banks customer-communications and dispute-handling practices generally, and because the Bureaus public complaint database serves an important function in documenting patterns of conduct by federally regulated financial institutions.

Complainant and Subject Institution Complainant : XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX ( formerly XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX ) Address : XXXX XXXX XXXX, XXXX XXXXXXXX XXXX XXXXXXXX Authorized representative : XXXX XXXX, XXXX ( XXXX ) Subject institution : U.S. Bank National Association Account : U.S. Bank commercial deposit account ending in XXXX, accessed through the U.S. Bank XXXX  ( commercial online banking ) platform.

Summary of the Complaint On XX/XX/XXXX, an impersonator obtained XXXX XXXX  credentials by means of a phishing telephone call that displayed a U.S. Bank-branded caller-ID and email communications sent from XXXX, U.S. Banks legitimate SinglePoint customer-service email address. 
XXXX XXXX promptly notified U.S. Bank by email and telephone the same day. On the XXXX Customer Service call, U.S. Bank told XXXX the Account had been flagged with the Fraud Department and that all transactions would be reviewed before execution. Approximately twenty hours later, U.S. Bank executed three of four unauthorized ACH payment orders that had been submitted by the impersonator using the compromised credentials, diverting {$53000.00}.

U.S. Bank closed its case on XX/XX/XXXX without findings, by way of a brief email from a local-branch employee that itself acknowledged the open question of what actions took place concerning the fraud conversation on XX/XX/XXXX with customer service. U.S. Banks formal response of XX/XX/XXXX from the banks XXXX XXXX XXXX, signed by XXXX XXXX declined reimbursement, characterized XXXX XX/XX/XXXX notice as merely an access issue, and pointed XXXX to the Security provisions of the Deposit Account Agreement. The response did not address or even acknowledge the central facts : the 20-hour notice window, the verbal assurance from the banks representative, and the banks execution of the orders despite that assurance.

Chronology of Events On the morning of XX/XX/XXXX, XXXX XXXX, an Accounts Payable employee of XXXX, received a telephone call. The caller-ID displayed U.S. Bank as the source of the call. The caller represented himself or herself to be a U.S. Bank employee, told XXXX XXXX that XXXX account had been the subject of fraudulent activity, and asked for login information to address the situation. During the call the caller also sent XXXX XXXX PIN-related emails purporting to come from XXXX the U.S. Bank XXXX customer-service email address. Believing the call to be legitimate, XXXX XXXX provided XXXX XXXX login credentials. 
At XXXX XXXX  CST on XX/XX/XXXX, XXXX XXXX, XXXX XXXX, emailed XXXX XXXX and XXXX XXXX U.S. Bank personnel known to her at U.S. Banks XXXX  branches to alert U.S. Bank to the suspicious activity. 
At XXXX XXXX CST on XX/XX/XXXX, XXXX XXXX also called U.S. Banks XXXX Customer Service line at XXXX. During that call, the U.S. Bank representative confirmed that the earlier call received by XXXX XXXX was an impersonator and informed XXXX XXXX that the U.S. Bank Fraud Department had flagged XXXX account and would review all of XXXX transactions before execution. At XXXX XXXX CST on XX/XX/XXXX, during the same call, the U.S. Bank representative emailed XXXX XXXX instructions to change XXXX password. XXXX XXXX did so immediately. 
At XXXX XXXX  CST on XX/XX/XXXX four minutes after XXXX XXXX call to XXXX XXXX XXXX began an unauthorized actor used the previously-obtained XXXX  credentials to submit four ACH payment orders totaling {$68000.00} against XXXX  account . The orders were not scheduled to execute that day ; they were scheduled for execution the following business day, XX/XX/XXXX. 
At XXXX XXXX  CST on XX/XX/XXXX, XXXX XXXX of the local U.S. Bank branch responded to XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX email, instructing her to call the XXXX Customer Service number a step XXXX XXXX had already independently taken six minutes earlier. 
At XXXX XXXX CST on XX/XX/XXXX, XXXX XXXX received written confirmation from U.S. Bank that XXXX  account credentials had been reset. As of this point in the timeline, U.S. Bank had : ( i ) been notified by email of the suspicious activity, ( ii ) been notified by telephone of the suspicious activity, ( iii ) confirmed by telephone that the originating call was an impersonator, ( iv ) represented that the Fraud Department had flagged the account and would review all transactions before execution, and ( v ) reset XXXX  account credentials. The four unauthorized payment orders sat in the banks queue overnight, scheduled for execution the next business day.

Notwithstanding all of the foregoing, on the morning of XX/XX/XXXX, U.S. Bank executed three of the four unauthorized payment orders, resulting in the diversion of {$53000.00} from XXXX account. The fourth order, in the amount of {$15000.00} payable to XXXX XXXX, was not completed because the receiving account had been closed. 
XXXX XXXX was monitoring the account on the morning of XX/XX/XXXX and saw the executions as they posted. At approximately XXXX XXXX CST on XX/XX/XXXX, she contacted U.S. Bank to report the unauthorized executions. U.S. Bank assigned ticket number XXXX and instructed her to wait for a call from the Fraud Department . No such call was received. At approximately XXXX XXXX  CST on XX/XX/XXXX, XXXX XXXX called U.S. Bank again and spoke with XXXX XXXX, who informed her for the first time that XXXX needed to submit an ACH Service Request Adjustment Form. XXXX XXXX completed and submitted the form the same day. Approximately one hour later, U.S. Bank confirmed by email that the {$15000.00} transfer to XXXX XXXX had not gone through because the receiving account had been closed ; the bank stated it was attempting to retrieve the remaining funds from the receiving banks. 
From XX/XX/XXXX forward, XXXX XXXX and XXXX XXXX ( then XXXX Chief Executive Officer ; XXXX XXXX transitioned to a consultant role effective XX/XX/XXXX, at which time XXXX XXXX became President of the company ) contacted U.S. Bank on at least ten occasions seeking information on the recovery effort and reimbursement of the diverted funds. 
At XXXX XXXX  on XX/XX/XXXX, XXXX XXXX of the local U.S. Bank branch emailed XXXX XXXX to inform XXXX  that U.S. Bank had been unable to recover the diverted funds. XXXX XXXX email stated : the ACH fraud department has closed the case. In the same email, XXXX XXXX also wrote : So, I guess the question is what actions took place concerning the fraud conversation on XX/XX/XXXX with customer service. That sentence from a U.S. Bank employee reflects the central unanswered question that frames this complaint. 
On XX/XX/XXXX, XXXX sent U.S. Bank a formal demand letter via certified mail and email, addressed to the Vice President of the XXXX Branch, the XXXX XXXX XXXX, and the General Counsel. The letter set out the chronology, demanded reimbursement of {$53000.00} within fourteen days, and put U.S. Bank on notice to preserve all related documents and communications. 
On XX/XX/XXXX, U.S. Bank responded by letter signed by XXXX XXXX, Senior Vice President and Head of Enterprise Complaints. The response declined reimbursement, characterized XXXX XXXX XX/XX/XXXX contact as merely an access issue, categorized the loss as an account takeover, and pointed XXXX to the Security provisions of the Deposit Account Agreement. The response did not address or even acknowledge ( i ) the approximately 20-hour notice period before the unauthorized executions, ( ii ) the bank representatives confirmation that the account had been flagged and would be subject to transaction review, or ( iii ) the banks execution of the orders despite that representation.

Note on the recovery facts : U.S. Banks XX/XX/XXXX letter states that one transaction in the amount of {$15000.00} was successfully reversed and credited. XXXX contemporaneous record reflected both in U.S. Banks own emails to XXXX XXXX on XX/XX/XXXX and in XXXX XX/XX/XXXX letter is that this transaction was never completed because the receiving bank account had been closed. The accounts of why {$15000.00} was not lost differ ; the operative fact for purposes of this complaint is that {$53000.00} remains unreimbursed.

Bureau-Relevant Aspects of the Complaint Smiths respectfully draws the Bureaus attention to the following aspects of U.S. Banks conduct that bear on the Bureaus general supervisory interest in consumer-facing communications and dispute-handling practices, even though the underlying account is commercial : ( a ) Inadequate investigation findings communicated to the customer. U.S. Banks XX/XX/XXXX closure communication contained no findings, no description of the banks investigative steps, and no explanation of the banks decision even as the banks own employee acknowledged in that communication that the relationship between the customers XX/XX/XXXX notice and the banks subsequent execution of the orders was an unanswered question. 
( b ) Mischaracterization of the customers notice in the formal response. The banks XX/XX/XXXX response describes XXXX XX/XX/XXXX contact as merely an access issue, which is inconsistent with the banks own contemporaneous conduct of flagging the account with the Fraud Department. Banks do not flag access issues with their fraud departments. The mischaracterization is the kind of dispute-response framing that the Bureaus complaint database is well-suited to document.

( c ) Use of the banks own customer-facing communications channels in the fraud. The fraud was effected through the impersonation of U.S. Banks caller-ID and the use of XXXX ( an actual U.S. Bank customer-service address ) to send PIN-related material to the customer. The Bureaus scrutiny of bank communications practices is well-served by examining whether U.S. Bank has adequate controls over the impersonation of its own outbound channels.

( d ) Public-database documentation. Even if the Bureau ultimately concludes that direct intervention is not appropriate for this commercial-account dispute, the Bureaus public complaint database serves an important function in documenting institutional conduct. XXXX respectfully requests that this complaint be accepted and made available in the database to that end.

Resolution Requested XXXX XXXX XXXX respectfully requests that the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau consider the following actions : ( a ) Open a formal complaint matter with U.S. Bank and require the bank to provide a written response that addresses the specific factual allegations summarized above, including the 20-hour notice window, the verbal assurance regarding transaction review , and the basis for executing the payment orders after the account had been flagged.

( b ) Request, as part of the agencys review, that U.S. Bank produce or preserve ( i ) the audio recording and any transcript of the XXXX XXXX  CST on XX/XX/XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX call, ( ii ) internal records reflecting the flagging of XXXX  account by the Fraud Department, and ( iii ) records reflecting why the four unauthorized payment orders were executed despite the flag.

( c ) Facilitate a review of U.S. Banks reimbursement decision so that, on the merits and on a complete record, the bank may reconsider its denial of reimbursement of the {$53000.00} diverted from XXXX account.

( d ) Such other action as the agency considers appropriate based on its review of the matter.

Enclosures The following supporting documents are submitted with this complaint : 1. Letter from XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX to U.S. Bank dated XX/XX/XXXX ( the original demand letter, with full chronology and document-preservation request ) ; 2. Letter from U.S. Bank to XXXX XXXX XXXX dated XX/XX/XXXX ( signed by XXXX XXXX, Senior Vice President and Head of Enterprise Complaints, denying reimbursement ) ; 3. Letter from TXXXX XXXX XXXX to U.S. Bank dated XX/XX/XXXX ( formal rejection of the banks position, renewed demand for reimbursement, and notice of these regulatory filings ).

Conclusion XXXX  submits this complaint to the Bureau and is filing parallel complaints with the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency ( U.S. Banks primary federal regulator ) and the Arkansas State Bank Department. XXXX is also contemporaneously transmitting to U.S. Bank a formal letter rejecting the banks XX/XX/XXXX position and renewing the demand for reimbursement of {$53000.00}.

Should the Bureau require additional documentation or wish to communicate with XXXX, please contact XXXX XXXX at XXXX. 

Respectfully submitted, XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. 
XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, XXXX XXXX 
XXXX XXXX

text:
I am filing this complaint against U.S. Bank regarding an unauthorized transaction on my checking account that resulted from their failure to deactivate an old debit card when a replacement card was issued.

Approximately two years ago, U.S. Bank issued me a new debit card. At no point was I informed that my old card number would remain active, and I no longer had possession of the old card. I assumed, reasonably, that the old card number had been deactivated upon issuance of the replacement which is standard industry practice. 

On XX/XX/year>, a transaction of {$330.00} was made at XXXX using my old debit card number. The transaction posted as completed on XX/XX/year>. I became aware of it that morning when I received an automated email alert from U.S. Bank, which I have configured to notify me of transactions exceeding {$150.00}.

I did not authorize this transaction. I do not have the old card and have not had possession of it for approximately two years. The only reason this fraud was possible is because U.S. Bank failed to deactivate the old card number when the replacement was issued. 

To further investigate, I used XXXX 's online receipt lookup tool, which requires the transaction date, card type, store location, last four digits of the card, and transaction amount. I searched every XXXX location in my area for this transaction and found no matching receipt. This strongly suggests the charge did not originate from a legitimate in-store purchase at any local XXXX. I have since contacted U.S. Bank and a fraud claim has been opened. However, I am filing this complaint for the following reasons : 1. U.S. Bank 's failure to deactivate a replaced debit card is an operational security failure that directly enabled unauthorized access to my account.

2. Consumers reasonably expect that when a bank issues a replacement card, the prior card number is closed. U.S. Bank 's failure to meet this expectation, and to communicate otherwise, constitutes an unfair practice that exposes customers to preventable fraud.

3. I want this incident on record in the event this is not an isolated failure and other U.S. Bank customers have experienced or may experience the same vulnerability.

I am requesting that the CFPB : - Ensure U.S. Bank fully resolves my fraud claim and returns the unauthorized {$330.00} promptly - Investigate whether U.S. Bank 's card replacement procedures meet federal consumer protection standards- Determine whether this failure has affected other U.S. Bank customers I am happy to provide additional documentation, account details, or transaction information upon request.
label: account_management
source_sub_issue: Problem using a debit or ATM card

text:
I am submitting this complaint regarding a Wells Fargo Everyday Checking account that I opened on XX/XX/year>, in response to a Wells Fargo promotional offer advertising a {$400.00} account-opening bonus. 

I opened the account online using the promotional code provided by Wells Fargo and deposited more than {$1000.00} into the account shortly after opening it. My understanding was that I was satisfying the funding requirement necessary to qualify for the promotional bonus. 

After waiting several months, I discovered that Wells Fargo had not paid the advertised bonus. Instead, Wells Fargo assessed XXXX monthly service fees totaling {$45.00}. 

When I contacted Wells Fargo, I was informed that although I had transferred more than {$1000.00} into the account, the funding method I used was not considered a " qualifying electronic deposit '' under the promotion 's terms. Wells Fargo stated that only certain ACH direct deposits or similar qualifying electronic payments would count. 

My concern is not simply the technical interpretation of the promotion. Rather, it is that I acted in good faith to fund the account with more than {$1000.00} and was never notified during the qualification period that Wells Fargo considered my deposit method ineligible. Had Wells Fargo provided timely notice that my deposit did not qualify, I could have corrected the issue, made a qualifying deposit, maintained the required balance, or closed the account before fees accumulated. 

Instead, Wells Fargo allowed monthly service fees to accrue while I reasonably believed I had met the promotion 's funding requirement. By the time I learned that Wells Fargo considered my deposit ineligible, I had already incurred multiple fees. 

After I complained, Wells Fargo refunded XXXX of the XXXX XXXX XXXX  monthly service fees but refused to refund the remaining {$15.00}, citing an internal courtesy refund policy. Wells Fargo also denied any consideration of the promotional bonus despite acknowledging that the promotional code was properly attached to the account and that I had deposited over {$1000.00} into the account. 

I believe this outcome is unfair to consumers. A reasonable customer who opens an account specifically for a promotional bonus and deposits more than {$1000.00} would expect to receive a clear warning if the funding method does not satisfy the promotion 's requirements. Instead, I received no such notice and was charged fees while believing I had complied with the promotion. 

I am requesting that Wells Fargo : Refund the remaining XXXX XXXX monthly service fee that was not reimbursed. 
Reconsider a goodwill credit of the advertised {$400.00} promotional bonus, or otherwise provide an equitable resolution in light of the fact that I funded the account in good faith and was not alerted that my deposit method was considered ineligible. 

I have already completed Wells Fargo 's internal complaint process and received a final response denying further relief.
label: account_management
source_sub_issue: Didn't receive terms that were advertised

text:
I am submitting this complaint regarding serious concerns involving my bank accounts and the handling of my account security. 

My previous bank accounts were compromised, resulting in the unexplained loss of approximately {$3000.00}. Despite my repeated requests, the bank has failed to provide any explanation regarding where these funds went. I instructed the bank to close the compromised accounts, and I was informed that they had been closed. However, when I requested written confirmation or account closure statements, the bank refused to provide them. 

Subsequently, I opened two new accounts and specifically requested that online banking access be disabled due to the security concerns surrounding my previous accounts. The banker assured me that online banking had been disabled on both accounts. Nevertheless, I continue to receive notifications indicating that monthly online statements are available for these accounts. 

In addition, my debit card has been randomly locking and unlocking without my authorization or knowledge. These unexplained changes have further heightened my concerns regarding the security and integrity of my accounts. I have contacted the bank on multiple occasions regarding these issues, but bank representatives continue to deny that online banking access exists and have failed to provide satisfactory explanations regarding the irregular activity affecting my accounts. 

Given the contradictory information, the unexplained disappearance of {$3000.00}, and the ongoing unauthorized changes to my debit card status, I have serious concerns regarding possible fraud, unauthorized access, and a breach of my account security. I further suspect that the institution 's failure to adequately secure my accounts and provide accurate information may be connected to the missing funds. 

I request an immediate and thorough investigation into the missing funds, written confirmation of all account closures, verification of whether online banking access remains active, a complete audit of all activity associated with my accounts, and an explanation regarding the repeated unauthorized locking and unlocking of my debit card. I also request that all records, communications, and electronic access logs relating to my accounts be preserved for investigative purposes.

text:
On or about XX/XX/year> ; I researched bank promotions that encouraged opening a new account. 

Chime had the best opportunity ( {$350.00} in bonus dollars over 3 months and no requirement of maintaining an excessively high balance ), acceptable terms, plus Chime had been rated pretty well. 

I had a side gig that utilized direct deposit so I initiated opening an account with Chime. 

Strangely, I was denied an account. 

I figured that I did something wrong, so I tried again. Still denied. 

I enlisted the help of a Chime agent and he entered the information. Denied again. The agent was baffled and couldnt explain it. He put me on hold to confer with a supervisor and returned with no new information. 

It was then that I asked the agent if the reason that I was denied an account was because I had declined signing up for the Chime credit card. Each time I had attempted to get a new account with Chime, I had refused to check the optional box that would include a linked credit card to my account. The agent assured me that it wasnt the case. 

Then, because I wanted to take advantage of the {$350.00} bonus money for opening a new account, I applied again - and this time I checked the box that would give me the credit card. 

I was approved for a new account. This made me suspicious, but I figured that I didnt have to use the card and I wanted the {$350.00} bonus, so I would roll with it. 

Today, XX/XX/XXXX, I decided to call the company and see where I stood with the promotion. 

The virtual agent could not see that I was in line for the said promotion. It wasnt easy, but I escalated the issue to a live agent. The virtual agent assured me that a human would not be able to help me further. 

It was right. The agent told me that I was not enrolled in the promotion and could not be switched into it at this point. 

She did try and sell me on a different promotion that required me to drum up business for them through a referral process. 

I told her that it sounded like a bait and switch scheme : draw me in with a bogus promotion and give me something else instead. 

I escalated to her supervisor- whom I shared all this same information with. 

He told me that I didnt qualify and that Chime was under no obligation to rectify the situation. I told him that in addition to the bait and switch scheme, and the fraudulent promotion, you force clients to take credit cards in order to qualify for an account. None of this is acceptable. He thanked me for contacting them and I told him that I would be contacting you.
label: account_management
source_sub_issue: Didn't receive terms that were advertised

text:
I am filing this complaint against Chime Financial , Inc. for unauthorized withdrawals from my account and the improper use of my funds. 

Chime has been removing money from my account without my permission and applying it toward balances or transactions that I did not authorize and was not aware of. I did not give consent for these withdrawals, and I did not agree to Chime taking funds from my account in this manner. 

These actions constitute unauthorized electronic fund transfers and are a violation of the Electronic Fund Transfer Act ( EFTA ) and Regulation E, which require consumer authorization before funds can be electronically withdrawn from a bank account. 

Chime does not have the right to remove money from my account without my consent. The funds that were taken were not authorized by me, and I did not approve any automatic deductions related to these transactions. 

Because these withdrawals were unauthorized, I am demanding the immediate reimbursement of all funds that were taken from my account without my consent. 

I am requesting the following resolution : Immediate refund of all unauthorized funds withdrawn from my account. 
Confirmation that no further unauthorized deductions will occur. 
A correction to my account reflecting the reimbursement of these funds. 

If Chime refuses to return these funds promptly, I am requesting that the CFPB review this matter as a potential violation of federal consumer protection laws related to unauthorized electronic fund transfers and unfair banking practices. 

The unauthorized removal of consumer funds is unacceptable, and I expect this issue to be resolved immediately through reimbursement of the funds taken from my account.
label: account_management

text:
I am submitting this complaint regarding Mountain America Credit Union, which has failed to properly address a case of identity theft and fraud involving accounts and loan applications opened in my name without my knowledge or consent. Despite providing a police report confirming the fraud and disputing the matter XXXX, Mountain America Credit Union has refused to correct the issue or remove the fraudulent activity from their records.

Incident Overview I discovered that fraudulent activity had occurred involving my personal information. Accounts and loan applications were submitted under my name without my authorization. 
After discovering this fraudulent activity, I immediately filed a police report confirming that I was a victim of identity theft. I provided Mountain America Credit Union with a copy of this police report and supporting documentation. 
Despite this evidence, I have disputed this matter XXXX, and Mountain America Credit Union has failed to take appropriate action to resolve the situation. They have refused to remove the fraudulent accounts and loan applications associated with my name. 
Because of this failure to correct the fraud, my personal information remains tied to accounts and loan applications that I did not authorize. 
Legal Violations Mountain America Credit Unions failure to properly investigate and remove fraudulent activity may violate the following consumer protection laws : Fair Credit Reporting Act ( FCRA ) 15 U.S.C. 1681 Under the FCRA, when a consumer disputes fraudulent accounts or information, the financial institution must conduct a reasonable investigation and correct or remove inaccurate information. Despite being provided with a police report confirming identity theft, Mountain America Credit Union has failed to correct the fraudulent accounts and loan applications. 
Fair Debt Collection Practices Act ( FDCPA ) 15 U.S.C. 1692 Consumers have the right to dispute fraudulent debts or accounts. By refusing to remove accounts and loan applications created through identity theft, Mountain America Credit Union has failed to properly address a valid dispute.

Identity Theft Utah Code 76-6-1102 The unauthorized use of my personal information to open accounts and apply for loans constitutes identity theft under Utah law.

text:
Date : XX/XX/XXXX Subject : Formal Dispute and Request for Reconsideration of Fraud Claim Debit Card XXXX in XXXX To Whom It May Concern, I am writing to formally dispute the denial of my fraud claim regarding unauthorized transactions totaling {$2500.00} from my checking account associated with my debit card ending in XXXX. 

I have been a member of Navy Federal Credit Union since XXXX and have maintained my accounts in good standing, including successfully paying off loans and a credit card with your institution. I have never had a history of overdrafts or fraudulent activity. 

On the date of the incident, I received a phone call that appeared to come from Navy Federal Credit Unions legitimate phone number. The caller represented themselves as a Navy Federal representative and instructed me to verify my account by providing a security code that was sent to my phone. Believing I was communicating directly with my financial institution, I complied with the instructions. 

Immediately after this interaction, unauthorized transactions occurred transferring funds from my Navy Federal account to my XXXX XXXX account. I was led to believe the transfer was part of a security process and that the funds would be returned to my account. At no time did I knowingly authorize fraudulent activity or intend to send funds to a third party. 

This situation appears to be a spoofing or impersonation scam, where an individual fraudulently represented themselves as Navy Federal staff. The fact that the call appeared to originate from Navy Federals number reasonably led me to believe the communication was legitimate. 

Under the Electronic Fund Transfer Act, consumers are protected from unauthorized electronic transfers. I respectfully request that Navy Federal reopen this investigation and conduct a thorough review of the following : Phone records related to the call made to me IP address and device information used to access my account Transaction logs and authentication records Any internal notes or recordings associated with this case Additionally, I am formally requesting all documentation used in the determination to deny my claim, as stated in the notification I received. 

Given the circumstances and the impersonation involved, I strongly believe this transaction qualifies as unauthorized fraud and should be reimbursed. 

Please treat this letter as a formal request for reconsideration and escalation of my case. I am also in the process of submitting complaints with the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and the National Credit Union Administration so that this matter may receive appropriate regulatory review. 

I appreciate your prompt attention to this matter and request a written response within the timeframe required by law. 

Sincerely, XXXX XXXX

On XX/XX/XXXX, Wells Fargo notified me that my accounts would be closed due to alleged unauthorized transfers. At that time, the funds in question were legitimate PPP proceeds deposited by XXXX XXXX XXXX. 

On XX/XX/XXXX, Wells Fargo informed me they were expecting a claim and would temporarily hold my funds for up to 60 days. They stated that if no claim was received, the funds would be returned to me. 

No claim was ever disclosed to me within that timeframe. 

Instead, Wells Fargo held my funds for nearly two years. In XXXX, I was informed that Wells Fargo sent {$15000.00} to XXXX XXXX XXXX  based on a Uniform Indemnification Agreement allegedly submitted on my behalf.

Despite multiple requests, Wells Fargo has refused to provide : - A copy of the Uniform Indemnification Agreement- The date it was received - The identity of the party who submitted it - Proof that I authorized any such request - Documentation of any prior claims deducted from my funds - A full accounting of the missing balance XXXX XXXX responses are inconsistent and contradictory. In XXXX, they refused to revisit the issue, stating no new information was provided, while still failing to provide any supporting documentation.

text:
To Whom It May Concern, I am filing a formal complaint against Bank of America regarding a billing dispute for my personal account ending in XXXX XX/XX/year>, I opened a claim ( Claim ID : XXXX ) for an unauthorized transaction in the amount of {$9600.00}. Under the Electronic Fund Transfer Act ( Regulation E, 12 CFR 1005.11 ), a financial institution is required to investigate a reported error and provide a provisional credit within 10 business days if the investigation is not yet complete.

As of today, more than 10 business days have passed since the claim was initiated. No provisional credit was ever issued. Furthermore, when I reached out via phone to inquire about the status after the 10-day window had closed, a Bank of America representative explicitly stated that Regulation E did not apply to my claim because of the high dollar amount.

This statement is factually incorrect and contradicts federal consumer protections. While the claim status was eventually updated to " complete '' on XX/XX/XXXX, the bank failed its procedural obligation to provide funds during the investigation period and provided misleading information regarding my rights. 

I am requesting that the CFPB investigate Bank of Americas handling of this claim and their failure to comply with Regulation Es provisional credit requirements. Attached is a screenshot of the claim status for your reference.Thank you for your assistance in this matter. 

Sincerely, XXXX XXXX
label: account_management

text:
I am writing to make a sincere complaint about a deposit that was made to my husband and Is joint checking account with Citibank on XX/XX/year>. 

We deposited our IRS refund check in the amount of {$11000.00}. We deposited this at the after hours teller and saw the transaction reflected in our online account immediately. 
However, after over a week of the check not clearing, we became concerned. 

My husband called Citibank customer service and was told to wait a few more business days. 

A few more business days later when it still did not clear, my husband went in person to the branch. 

The branch XXXX found that the check had not been properly endorsed in that it turns out that both my husband and I needed to endorse it since it was in both our names and only XXXX of us had endorsed it. We were told that the check was being mailed back to us and all we needed to do was bring it back to the bank properly endorsed. We were told this could take up to XXXX business days. 

XXXX business days later when the check still did not arrive, we went back to the bank. 
The branch XXXX did their best, but could not figure out what had happened to the check. She submitted an investigation to find out what happened to the check and we were told that in XXXX more business days we would be given a provisional credit for the amount of the check if it still was not found. 

Several days later we received a letter from Citibank saying that we were going to receive the provisional credit for {$11000.00} ( supposedly the extra {$50.00} were to cover fees that weve incurred ), while they completed their investigation. We were told that we could freely use these funds in the meantime. 

As you might XXXX, we had already been waiting with anticipation for our IRS refund check and already quite a bit of time had passed. We needed this money to pay credit card bills that already have extremely high interest rates, XXXX of those credit cards is a Citibank credit card. 

So we paid our bills with a large part of this credited amount, thinking that we would receive a resolution very soon with our check. 

However, yesterday on XX/XX/year>, we received a letter from Citibank saying that they had included concluded their investigation and determined that the check had already been sent to us and they were removing the provisional credit. 

We checked our bank account and sure enough, our bank account- for the first time ever - was in the negative for over {$4000.00}. The letter said the credit would be removed in XXXX business days however, they did not wait XXXX business days, it has already been removed. 

And of course, we still do not have our check. 
And in the meantime, we have automated bill payments to our checking account that we are not sure how we can handle, in addition to credit card payments. 

My husband was quite upset and called Citibank customer service. They were less than no help. All they did is tell my husband that he should ask the IRS for a new check. They could not give him any information as to when the check was actually sent out. They were also quite rude and hung up on him. 

As you can imagine, if we had known from the beginning that they did not have the check at all, we would have just asked the IRS to give us a replacement check in the first place. But by now quite a lot of of time has passed, and we are concerned that the check may have been used fraudulently by someone within the banking system. 

My husband again went in person to the branch and the branch XXXX tried their best to help, but she said that she had very limited options. 
She again started an investigation and we were again told that we must wait a few more business days for a response. 

As you can imagine, in the meantime, we are in a terrible situation. We used funds that should be ours to pay very high credit card bills with high interest. We are now over {$4000.00} in the negative, without any idea as to when we will receive a resolution. 

My husband and I both work full-time, but we make modest salaries and we can not afford to make up for the shortfall in the meantime. We also have a young child with special needs and tons of expenses for her care. 

Furthermore, we have already spent so much time making phone calls back-and-forth to Citibank as well as taking time off from work to go in person to the branch. 

Not to mention, over a month where we were not able to pay a significant amount for our credit card debt and thus incurring even more interest fees. 
And we still do not know what has happened to our check. 

I have since called the IRS to ask for a replacement in the hopes that it already hasnt been deposited fraudulently however, despite multiple calls, I was unable to speak to a representative to find out the status. Through their automated system, I was told that I will be mailed a copy of form XXXX. 
Assuming that the check has not already been fraudulently deposited, as you may know, a replacement check could take quite a while to arrive. 

We are asking for Citibank to take responsibility for the whereabouts of this check, to again give us the provisional amount until we can receive a replacement check, whenever that might be. 

In addition, we want to be compensated for the time, extreme stress and high interest fees that we have incurred because of their negligence. I thank you sincerely for your attention to this matter.
label: account_management

I am filing this complaint as a follow-up to my previous CFPB complaint ( Ref No : XXXX, American Express Ref : XXXX XXXX regarding the forfeiture of approximately XXXX Membership Rewards points. 
American Express XXXX response to my initial complaint contains a critical factual error that I believe requires further review. 
The full sequence of events I cancelled my Business Gold Card ending in XXXX on XX/XX/XXXX, and transferred my Membership Rewards points to my first Rewards Checking account ending in XXXX. 
My first Rewards Checking account ending in XXXX was closed by American Express on XX/XX/XXXX. 
In XX/XX/XXXX, I opened a second American Express Checking account. After opening this account, I was able to successfully transfer my Membership Rewards points from the first closed checking account into my second checking account. This transfer was processed and approved by American Express XXXX own system. 
My approximately XXXX Membership Rewards points were then visible and accessible in my second checking account. In XX/XX/XXXX, when I attempted to link my new XXXX XXXX XXXX ending in XXXX to my second checking account, my points suddenly disappeared. American Express has never explained why this linking action caused the points to vanish. This is an additional issue that their response completely failed to address. 

American Express XXXX response ignores the second checking account entirely In their response, American Express stated that " bank statements do not show an active Membership Rewards points balance after the closure. '' This statement refers only to the Membership Rewards account and the first closed checking account. It completely ignores the existence of my second checking account, where the points were actively displayed and accessible. 
The reason the Membership Rewards account showed no balance is because all of my points had been transferred to and were showing in my second checking account. This is not evidence that the points were forfeited it is simply how the points were being tracked in their system. 
American Express XXXX own system allowed the transfer after the alleged forfeiture date If my points had truly been forfeited when my first checking account was closed on XX/XX/XXXX, American Express XXXX own system should not have allowed me to transfer those points to my second checking account afterward. American Express admitted they failed to notify me In their written response, American Express stated : " we did not inform you about points forfeitures during that time. I am filing this complaint regarding check fraud that Bank of America has refused to properly resolve. 

In XX/XX/XXXX, I issued a single legitimate check to an employee for the amount of {$750.00} check number XXXX. Approximately one year later, I discovered that this check had been fraudulently duplicated and/or altered and was negotiated multiple times without my authorization. A total of three transactions were processed against my account stemming from this one check using the following check numbers : Check number : XXXX. XXXX {$750.00}. Date : XX/XX/XXXX Check number : XXXX. XXXX {$750.00}. Date : XX/XX/XXXX Check number : ( no number ). XXXX {$750.00}. Date : XX/XX/XXXX The XXXX checks look exactly the same as the one I legitimate issued. 

I did not authorize these additional transactions under any circumstances. This is a clear case of check fraud involving duplicate and/or altered instruments. 

When I brought this to Bank of Americas attention, they denied my claim based solely on the amount of time that had passed. However, this is not a standard late-reported transaction. This matter involves fraud and improper payment of unauthorized items. Under applicable banking rules, including Uniform Commercial Code ( UCC ) provisions, a bank should not charge a customers account for altered, forged, or duplicate checks. 

Despite this, Bank of America has failed to : Reimburse the unauthorized transactions Provide a satisfactory explanation of how the same check was processed multiple times Demonstrate that proper fraud prevention procedures were followed I am requesting that Bank of America : Reimburse all unauthorized transactions related to this fraudulent activity totaling : {$2200.00} Provide copies of the front and back of all negotiated checks, including endorsements Conduct and share the results of a proper investigation into how these items were processed This situation represents both financial harm and a failure to protect my account from fraudulent activity. I am requesting the CFPBs assistance in ensuring this matter is properly investigated and resolved.
label: account_management

text:
I am submitting this complaint to formally document repeated operational failures by U.S. Bank involving high-value check deposits and to ensure the record clearly reflects that I bear no responsibility or fault in these events. 

I have been a U.S. Bank customer since XXXX. 

First incident On XX/XX/XXXX, I deposited a {$380000.00} check payable to my daughter at the XXXX branch, XXXX  XXXX. A branch employee handled the transaction and recommended opening a savings account for my daughter to receive the funds. The account later appeared in my online banking view under my Power of Attorney access, and I reasonably believed the deposit was completed correctly.

In the following weeks, my daughter never saw the account or funds in her own banking view. After significant delay, I contacted the payer, who confirmed the check had been withdrawn and provided the deposited check image. When I returned to the branch, I was informed that the funds had been deposited back into a newly opened account ( ending with XXXX ) belonging to the payer, with me listed as PoA. The named payee received nothing. No corrective action, apology, or remediation was offered, and I was only instructed to resolve the situation privately with the payer. 

Second incident Because of the first failure, the payer reissued a replacement check, which I deposited at a different branch on XX/XX/XXXX. During this transaction, the {$380000.00} check was entered into the system as {$380.00}. This error was only caught because I reviewed the receipt right before leaving the counter. The cashier corrected it in the system without saying anything. And later I found it shows nothing in my banking view. Had it not been discovered immediately, it would have resulted in further loss of time and potentially severe complications. 

Systemic concern These two incidents occurred within a short time period, involved the same high-value amount, and arose from separate transactions and staff. Together, they demonstrate a breakdown in operational accuracy and verification controls rather than isolated human error. At no point did I contribute to or cause these errors. 

I am submitting this complaint to ensure that the record accurately reflects repeated bank operational failures and that I bear no responsibility or fault. I request that U.S. Bank provide a written response addressing root cause, accountability, and corrective actions.

text:
I am filing a complaint against BancFirst regarding their handling of unauthorized electronic fund transfers under Regulation E. 

On XX/XX/year>, six ( 6 ) unauthorized XXXX XXXX transactions totaling {$2100.00} were processed from my account to an individual I do not know. I did not initiate, authorize, or benefit from these transactions. 

The bank initially issued provisional credit on XX/XX/year>. On XX/XX/year>, I was notified that my claim was denied and the provisional credit would be reversed. 

On XX/XX/year>, I contacted BancFirst and spoke with an officer ( XXXX XXXX to understand the basis of the denial. I was advised that the matter was not the banks responsibility and that my dispute should be directed to XXXX XXXX. I explained that I believed I was protected under Regulation E through my financial institution. 

I requested documentation supporting their determination. As of XX/XX/year>, I had not received any documentation and submitted a written request through the banks website, as no email contact was provided in the denial notice. 

As of XX/XX/year>, I still had not received documentation and had to call customer service to obtain it. I was then transferred to a supervisor who advised they could not discuss my case and instructed me not to respond via email. I nevertheless followed up via email requesting clarification as to how the bank determined that I authorized the transactions. I was told my inquiry would be forwarded to the investigations team, but I have received no further response. 

The documentation I eventually received includes transaction receipts and a statement indicating that a passcode ( PIN ) was used and that the transaction was authenticated using a device and IP address. 

However, this documentation does not establish that I authorized the transactions. Authentication ( such as use of a passcode ) is not equivalent to authorization by the account holder under Regulation E. 

Additional concerns include : -The IP address provided does not align with my location nor is my active IP address -I do not use a PIN for these transactions and instead rely on biometric authentication -While I do have possession of the device originally associated with my account, I had not actively used the application and did not initiate or authorize these transactions. Possession of a device does not establish that I authorized the transfers Further, the banks fraud monitoring practices appear inconsistent. I was contacted regarding two transactions, which were blocked after I confirmed they were unauthorized, yet seven back-to-back transactions to the same recipient were allowed to process without interruption, totaling {$2100.00}. 

Separately, on XX/XX/year>, my debit card was blocked after two legitimate transactions from a known vendor, without any prior notification or attempt to contact me. When I called, they advised they " flagged '' my card due to two back-to- back transactions. This raises additional concerns regarding the consistency and reliability of the banks fraud detection and response practices. 

Overall, BancFirst : -Failed to demonstrate that I authorized the transactions -Relied solely on authentication factors rather than establishing authorization -Failed to provide timely and clear documentation upon request -Provided inconsistent fraud monitoring and response I am requesting that BancFirst : -Reconsider its determination -Recredit the {$2100.00} to my account -Ensure proper application of Regulation E protections
label: account_management

Despite this, the bank misclassified the dispute as a billing error and denied the claim based on an incorrect XXXX billing dispute standard. This reflects a failure to apply the appropriate fraud/scam investigation framework. 

I submitted supporting documentation ( contract, communications, and financial loss evidence ), but the banks denial does not demonstrate meaningful review or explanation of this evidence. 

Additionally, the merchant ( Safe Travels ) represented to the Texas Attorney General that I had been refunded through dispute resolution, after which the Attorney General closed the matter. However, I did not receive a clear, final, verifiable resolution consistent with that representation, raising concerns about the accuracy of information relied upon in downstream dispute handling. 

I am requesting : XXXX. Immediate reclassification of the claim under fraud/scam procedures XXXX. Full reconsideration of the denial based on coercion and misrepresentation XXXX. A detailed explanation of how my evidence was reviewed and why it was disregarded XXXX Review of the merchants reported refund status and its impact on prior determinations The banks handling reflects procedural misapplication, inadequate investigation, and reliance on an incorrect dispute classification that prevented fair adjudication of the claim.
