text:
I am writing in response to PNC Banks letter regarding my complaint. Your response does not address the core issues I raised and instead relies on internal calculations that contradict the balances that were actually displayed to me. 

My complaint is specifically about the balance shown in my account on XX/XX/year>. On that date, after receiving a {$600.00} XXXX deposit and sending {$190.00}, my account displayed a balance of {$570.00}. I have a timestamped screenshot confirming this. 

In your response, you claim that my starting balance on XX/XX/year> was {$380.00} and base your explanation on that figure. However, this does not explain how my account displayed a balance of {$570.00} on that same day. If your stated starting balance were accurate, it would not be possible for my account to reflect the balance shown in my screenshot. 

Instead of addressing this discrepancy, your response shifts focus to XX/XX/year>. My complaint is not about XX/XX/XXXX. It is about the accuracy of the balance displayed to me on XX/XX/XXXX. Referencing transactions from the following day does not resolve or explain the issue I raised. 

There is also an ongoing issue with the way my balances are presented. My transaction history consistently shows a higher balance than what I actually have available, even in situations where transactions later post and cause my account to become negative. This creates a misleading representation of my actual funds. 

You state that transaction history may not always reflect real time activity. However, this directly contradicts both my experience and your own published information. According to PNC Banks website, available balances and email alerts reflect real time activity. In my case, my available balance and email alerts have consistently matched each other, while my transaction history reflects higher amounts that do not align with my actual spending power. 

This is why I am confused as to why your response continues to rely on transaction history as the accurate source of information, when your own disclosures indicate that it is not always real time. If the balance I am shown in real time is not accurate, that is a serious issue that has not been addressed. 

Your letter also states that I opted into overdraft coverage on XX/XX/year>. I dispute this and request verifiable documentation showing when and how this authorization was obtained. I immediately opted out of overdraft coverage on XX/XX/year> because I realized I had enabled it by mistake, and I have the email confirmation to prove this. Therefore, your claim that I had overdraft coverage is still invalid. If I did not validly opt in, transactions that resulted in a negative balance should not have been approved. 

At this time, your response fails to explain how my account displayed a balance of {$570.00} on XX/XX/year>, why your stated starting balance does not align with the balance shown to me, why my complaint about XX/XX/XXXX was addressed using XX/XX/XXXX activity, and why my transaction history consistently reflects higher balances than my actual available funds. 

I am requesting a detailed, timestamped transaction ledger for XX/XX/year>, including all pending and posted transactions, as well as documentation supporting your claim regarding overdraft opt in and the discrepancies between transaction history and available balance. I requested for a complete ledger not the inaccurate information you provided. Not providing a complete ledger leads me to believe that you know Im right but youre doing everything in your power to prevent me from getting whats owed to me. 

Until these issues are fully addressed with clear documentation, I do not consider this matter resolved.

text:
Complaint Details I am submitting this complaint against [ regarding repeated improper denials/closures of my claims for unauthorized electronic fund transfers on my Everyday Checking account ending in XXXX ( debit card ending in XXXX ). These were fraudulent transactions I did not authorize, initiate, or benefit from. The bank has denied or closed four separate claims without providing a sufficient written explanation of their investigation, in violation of Regulation E ( 12 CFR 1005.11 ) error resolution procedures. I am requesting full reversal of all charges, refund of all related overdraft/NSF fees ( no matter when incurred ), and confirmation that proper procedures were followed.

Timeline and Specific Claims ( all on the same checking account/card ) : 1. Claim # XXXX : {$900.00} unauthorized purchase authorized on XX/XX/XXXX at XXXX ( posted XXXX ). Closed on XX/XX/XXXX with message : After careful review, your claim has been declined. 
2. Claim # XXXX : Two unauthorized recurring payments totaling {$92.00} XXXX  * {$24.00} ( authorized XX/XX/XXXX ) and XXXX XXXX {$67.00} ( authorized XX/XX/XXXX ), both marked Removed. Closed on XX/XX/XXXX and declined. 
3. Claim # XXXX : {$140.00} unauthorized purchase authorized on XX/XX/XXXX at XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, FL. Closed on XX/XX/XXXX as Closed. 
4. Claim # XXXX : {$200.00} unauthorized purchase authorized on XX/XX/XXXX at XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, FL. Closed on XX/XX/XXXX as Closed. 
These represent a pattern of unauthorized activity totaling approximately $ XXXX in the provided claims alone, with my overall losses from related unauthorized transactions approaching {$4000.00}. I promptly reported each as unauthorized/fraudulent and did not authorize, set up, or consent to any of these transactions or recurring payments. 
Violations of Regulation E ( 12 CFR Part 1005 ) : Under 1005.11 ( Procedures for Resolving Errors ), upon receiving notice of an alleged unauthorized EFT, the bank must promptly investigate, determine whether an error occurred within 10 business days ( or 20 for new accounts ), and report the results in writing. If an error ( unauthorized transfer ) occurred, the bank must correct it within one business day this includes refunding the full amount of the unauthorized transfers and refunding any fees imposed by the institution as a result ( including overdraft or NSF fees caused by the unauthorized activity ).

The burden of proof is on the bank ( XXXX and related interpretations ) to establish that the transfers were authorized by me. They can not deny claims solely based on lack of a police report, failure to contact merchants, or without providing a detailed written explanation of their findings and the evidence relied upon. 

Any overdraft or related fees resulting from these unauthorized transactions must also be reversed and refunded as part of error correction under Reg E official interpretations ( see XXXX commentary on crediting interest and refunding fees ). I am not responsible for fees triggered by transactions I did not do.
label: fees_interest

Charging overdraft fees under these circumstances is misleading, unacceptable, and inconsistent with fair and transparent banking practices. 

This pattern is consistent with the widely scrutinized authorize positive, settle negative overdraft practice, which has been the subject of significant regulatory enforcement and litigation. If Security Service Federal Credit Union is utilizing posting or settlement practices that generate overdraft fees after transactions were approved with a positive balance, such conduct may constitute an unfair or deceptive practice.

text:
Subject : Formal Complaint Against Golden 1 Credit Union Unauthorized Transaction / Fraud Dispute Denial I am filing a formal complaint against Golden 1 Credit Union regarding the improper handling of a fraud dispute on my account. 

On XX/XX/year>, a transaction in the amount of {$990.00} was made from my account without my authorization. This transaction was not initiated, approved, or knowingly participated in by me. I later discovered that my ex-partner accessed my account and transferred funds to another individual without my consent.

I promptly reported this transaction to Golden 1 as unauthorized fraud. The credit union issued a provisional credit during the investigation ; however, they later denied my dispute, claiming that I participated in the transaction and agreed to the terms. This conclusion is incorrect. 

At no point did I authorize this transfer, nor did I knowingly approve any terms related to it. The fact that another individual had access to my account does not constitute authorization under federal consumer protection laws. I believe Golden 1 failed to properly evaluate the circumstances of unauthorized access and instead relied solely on superficial transaction data. 

Additionally, I was not provided with sufficient documentation or evidence supporting their decision, nor was I given a fair opportunity to challenge their findings. 

Golden XXXX has stated they will reverse the provisional credit of {$990.00} on XX/XX/year>, which will cause financial hardship and reflects an unjust outcome given the fraudulent nature of the transaction. 

I am requesting : 1. A full reinvestigation of this matter as an unauthorized fraud claim 2. A detailed explanation and documentation of all evidence used in denying my dispute 3. Immediate restoration of the provisional credit while the investigation is properly conducted I believe my rights under federal regulations governing unauthorized electronic fund transfers have not been upheld. 

Please investigate this matter and require Golden 1 Credit Union to correct this improper decision. 

Case # XXXX

text:
This complaint documents willful, repeated violations of the Fair Credit Reporting Act ( FCRA ), 15 U.S.C. 1681 et seq., and the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act ( FDCPA ), 15 U.S.C. 1692 et seq. The subject company operates as a third-party contingency collector ( not a debt purchaser or assignee ) for XXXX XXXX XXXXXXXX , yet has furnished derogatory information to XXXX XXXX and XXXX without legal ownership of the debt or specific assignment authority to report.

Despite multiple formal disputes, the collector has : ( 1 ) recycled the same legally insufficient " validation '' document that explicitly disclaims being a final accounting ; ( 2 ) deliberately obstructed my ability to submit exculpatory evidence by refusing to provide any submission mechanism ; ( 3 ) reported inaccurate information causing severe financial harm; and ( 4 ) violated the fundamental requirement that only debt owners or properly authorized assignees may report to consumer reporting agencies. 
DETAILED FACTUAL ALLEGATIONS I. FRAUDULENT " VALIDATION '' REPETITIVE PROVISION OF LEGALLY DEFECTIVE DOCUMENTATION Following multiple written disputes ( via certified mail and electronic means ), this collector has repeatedly provided the identical, legally insufficient documentation : a XX/XX/year> " Statement of Security Deposit Accounts '' issued by XXXX HXXXX XXXX. This document explicitly states : " This may NOT be a final accounting statement. '' Under North Carolina General Statutes 42-50 et seq., landlords must provide a final, itemized accounting within 30 days of lease termination. By attempting to validate a debt using a document that the original creditor expressly admitted was not final, and by refusing to provide the actual final accounting required by law, the collector has : Violated 15 U.S.C. 1692e ( 2 ) ( A ) False representation of the legal status of the debt Violated 15 U.S.C. 1681s-2 ( a ) ( 1 ) ( A ) Furnishing information known to be inaccurate or with reckless disregard for accuracy Violated 15 U.S.C. 1681e ( b ) Failing to follow reasonable procedures to assure maximum possible accuracy The collector has failed to produce : Proof of assignment or legal ownership permitting credit reporting The statutorily required final accounting Itemized receipts substantiating the alleged {$2100.00} balance Justification for the one-year delay between move-out and collection placement II. DELIBERATE OBSTRUCTION REFUSAL TO PROVIDE EVIDENCE SUBMISSION MECHANISM In direct contravention of FCRA investigation requirements, this collector has refused to provide any physical mailing address, email address, fax number, or secure portal where I may submit critical evidence necessary for a reasonable investigation. This obstruction includes denial of access to submit : Email correspondence demonstrating repeated attempts to contact XXXX XXXX XXXX  to resolve this matter prior to collection placement ; Documentary evidence that XXXXXXXX XXXX XXXX  refused to accept payment when I tendered full payment of undisputed amounts, thereby manufacturing a default through their own refusal to accept lawful payment ; Contemporaneous dispute records showing the XX/XX/year> statement was immediately c

ontested and never corrected to a final accounting as required by N.C.G.S. 42-50. By denying me the mechanism to submit evidence for their purported " investigation '' while simultaneously reporting inaccurate information to credit bureaus, the collector has violated 15 U.S.C. 1681i ( a ) ( 1 ) ( A ) ( failure to conduct reasonable investigation ) and 15 U.S.C. 1692g ( b ) ( failure to verify disputed debts ). III. UNAUTHORIZED CREDIT REPORTING BY CONTINGENCY COLLECTOR WITHOUT DEBT OWNERSHIP This constitutes the most egregious violation : The collector is operating as a mere
contingency agent for XXXXXXXX XXXX XXXXXXXXnot a debt purchaser, assignee, or creditor. Under the FCRA and XXXX reporting standards, a collector may only report to credit bureaus if : It owns the debt ( purchased outright ) ; OR It has specific, documented written authority from the creditor to report on its behalf, with clear assignment of rights to report as a " furnisher. '' This collector has neither. It is collecting on a contingency fee basis only, meaning it receives payment only if it collects, but owns no legal interest in the debt itself. By reporting derogatory tradelines without ownership or specific assignment authority, the collector is : Falsely representing its legal status under 15 U.S.C. 1692e ( false representation of the character, amount, or legal status of any debt ) Furnishing information it has no legal right to furnish under 15 U.S.C. 1681s-2 ( a ) ( 1 ) ( A ) Engaging in deceptive collection practices prohibited under 12 C.F.R. 1006.18 Third-party collectors operating on contingency lack standing to report to credit bureaus. Only the original creditor or a debt owner/assignee with written reporting authority may furnish information. This collector 's reporting is ultra vires and constitutes defamation per se.

IV. BAD FAITH MANUFACTURE OF DEFAULT THROUGH REFUSAL TO ACCEPT PAYMENT As documented in email correspondence ( which the collector refuses to accept despite its relevance ), XXXXXXXX XXXX XXXX willfully refused to accept payment when I attempted to tender full payment of undisputed amounts prior to collection placement. This constitutes unclean hands and bad faith, rendering any subsequent collection efforts fraudulent. A creditor can not refuse lawful tender, convert the refused payment into a " default, '' and then damage a consumer 's credit through a third-party agent.

V. TANGIBLE FINANCIAL HARM The continued reporting of this unsubstantiated, disputed debt by an unauthorized furnisher has caused : Denial of mortgage pre-approval and adverse interest rate adjustments Employment background check failures Auto loan denials Severe emotional distress, XXXX, and financial instability These damages are direct and proximate results of the collector 's
willful non-compliance with federal law.

LEGAL BASIS FOR ENFORCEMENT ACTION Table Violation Statutory Citation Specific Conduct Unauthorized Furnishing 15 U.S.C. 1681s-2 ( a ) ( 1 ) ( A ) Reporting as contingency agent without debt ownership or assignment authority Failure to Verify 15 U.S.C. 1681i ( a ) ( 1 ) ( A ) Refusing to accept exculpatory evidence ; failure to conduct reasonable investigation False Representation 15 U.S.C. 1692e ( 2 ) ( A ) Representing that non-final statement constitutes validation ; claiming authority to report Obstruction of Dispute Process 15 U.S.C. 1692g ( b ) Denying mechanism to submit dispute evidence Inaccurate Reporting 15 U.S.C. 1681e ( b ) Reporting disputed, unverified, inaccurate information Unfair Practice 15 U.S.C. 1692f Attempting to collect via credit reporting while refusing to accept evidence of payment refusal REQUESTED RESOLUTION I demand the following mandatory corrective actions within 15 days of this complaint : IMMEDIATE PERMANENT DELETION of this tradeline from XXXX XXXX XXXX, and all other consumer reporting agencies. This deletion must be reported as " Deleted due to lack of furnisher authority '' or similar accurate coding, not " Disputed by consumer. '' FORMAL WRITTEN ACKNOWLEDGMENT from the collector confirming that it understands third-party contingency collectors without debt ownership or specific assignment authority are prohibited from reporting to credit bureaus under the FCRA and XXXX standards .

CESSATION OF ALL COLLECTION ACTIVITIES pending proof of legal authority to collect and report this debt.

PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTATION showing the collector 's authority to report, including : ( a ) debt purchase agreement OR ( b ) written assignment from XXXX XXXX specifically authorizing credit reporting. 
CORRECTIVE ACTION TO XXXX XXXX  requiring the original creditor to provide the final accounting required by N.C.G.S. 42-50 or cease collection efforts.

REQUEST FOR ESCALATION TO ENFORCEMENT AUTHORITIES I respectfully request that the CFPB immediately refer this matter to : The North Carolina Attorney General, Consumer Protection Division, for investigation of unlicensed collection practices and violations of the North Carolina Debt Collection Act ( N.C.G.S. 58-70-1 et seq. ) ; The Federal Trade Commission ( FTC ), for systematic FCRA enforcement action against this collector 's practice of unauthorized reporting by contingency agents ; The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau 's Enforcement Division, for potential consent order proceedings given the willful nature of these violations and the collector 's refusal to correct conduct despite multiple disputes. 
The 15-day deadline for resolution is non-negotiable. Each day this unauthorized tradeline remains on my credit reports constitutes ongoing, compounding damages and continued willful violation of federal law.

I reserve all rights to pursue private litigation under 15 U.S.C. 1681n ( willful FCRA violations ), 15 U.S.C. 1692k ( FDCPA statutory damages ), and applicable state law for defamation and unfair trade practices.

GOVERN YOURSELVES ACCORDINGLY.

Respectfully submitted,

text:
I am filing this complaint against OnePay regarding their handling of a fraud dispute and their failure to issue a required provisional credit within the appropriate timeframe. 

In XXXX, I reported unauthorized transactions totaling approximately {$2200.00} after my phone and cards were stolen. The unauthorized individual used my device and accessed my accounts without my permission. Most importantly, OnePay has failed to issue a provisional credit within the required timeframe. According to their own terms and standard consumer protection requirements, if an investigation exceeds 10 business days, a provisional credit must be issued while the investigation continues.

This timeframe has already been exceeded. When I contacted OnePay, I was told : * The dispute was re-opened, and therefore no timeline applies * There is currently no estimated date for when the provisional credit will be issued This is unacceptable. Reopening a dispute does not eliminate the requirement to issue a provisional credit, and their refusal to provide a date or comply with this requirement is a clear delay of funds that I am entitled to while the investigation is ongoing.

I am requesting the CFPB to investigate OnePays failure to : 1. Issue a provisional credit within the required timeframe 2. Properly evaluate evidence of unauthorized transactions 3. Provide clear and compliant dispute resolution timelines This situation has caused significant financial hardship, and I am seeking immediate resolution, including the issuance of my provisional credit and proper handling of my fraud claim.

text:
I dispute Wells Fargos XX/XX/XXXX response. The banks response is insufficient, conclusory, and does not resolve my complaint. I dispute both the denial of my claim and Wells Fargos refusal to refund the overdraft fees tied to the disputed activity. 

Wells Fargo says it completed research, but its letter does not show a reasonable, documented investigation of the disputed transfers. Under the Electronic Fund Transfer Act and Regulation E, once a consumer gives timely notice of an alleged error, the financial institution must investigate the alleged error, determine whether an error occurred, and report the results to the consumer within the required time. If the institution can not complete the investigation within 10 business days, it may use provisional credit, but the investigation still must be concluded within the applicable regulatory period, and the consumer must have full use of the provisionally credited funds during the investigation. 15 U.S.C. 1693f ( a ), ( c ).

Wells Fargos response does not provide the factual basis necessary to justify denial. The letter says only that Wells Fargo did not identify evidence that the payment was made by an alternate method, could not locate charges on an alternate Wells Fargo account, and found the disputed transactions consistent with prior undisputed activity. Those are generalized conclusions, not a transaction-specific explanation showing what records were reviewed, what merchant or network data was obtained, what account evidence was compared, or how Wells Fargo determined that the disputed transactions were actually authorized by me. Regulation E requires a real investigation and a written explanation of findings if the bank concludes no error occurred. 12 C.F.R. 1005.11 ( c ), ( d ).

The CFPBs official commentary also makes clear that a financial institution must begin its investigation promptly upon receipt of an oral notice and may not delay until it receives written confirmation. It also states that if an error occurred, the institution must correct the error, including refunding any fees imposed by the institution, where applicable. 12 C.F.R. Part 1005, Official Interpretations to 1005.11 ( c ).

I also dispute Wells Fargos apparent effort to rely on prior undisputed activity, receipt of benefit, or a broad transaction pattern instead of proving authorization of the disputed transfers. Under the Electronic Fund Transfer Act and Regulation E, consumer liability for unauthorized electronic fund transfers is limited by law, and the bank must comply with Regulation E before imposing liability on the consumer for an unauthorized EFT claim. 15 U.S.C. 1693g ( a ) ; 12 C.F.R. 1005.6 ( b ) ; Official Interpretations to 1005.11 ( b ). 
The response is also deficient because it does not provide the documentation underlying Wells Fargos determination. If a financial institution determines that no error occurred, it must provide an explanation of its findings, include notice of the consumers right to request the documents relied upon, and, upon request, promptly provide reproductions of the documents it relied on. 15 U.S.C. 1693f ( d ) ; 12 C.F.R. 1005.11 ( d ) ( 1 ). The CFPBs official commentary further states that the institution must provide copies in understandable form. 

I am therefore formally requesting that Wells Fargo produce all documents and data it relied upon in denying my claim and reversing the provisional credit, including but not limited to internal claim notes, transaction logs, account comparisons, merchant information, network records, screenshots, and any other material used to conclude that no error occurred. Regulation E expressly provides that those documents must be produced upon request. 

I also dispute Wells Fargos refusal to refund the overdraft fees listed in its own response. Wells Fargo identified XXXX overdraft fees of {$35.00} each, totaling {$420.00}. Those fees are : Account ending in XXXX : XXXX payment posted XX/XX/XXXX {$35.00} fee applied XX/XX/XXXX XXXX XXXX posted XX/XX/XXXX {$35.00} fee applied XX/XX/XXXX Account ending in XXXX : XXXXXXXX XXXX XXXXXXXX XXXX XXXX posted XX/XX/XXXX {$35.00} fee applied XX/XX/XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. XXXX XXXX posted XX/XX/XXXX {$35.00} fee applied XX/XX/XXXX XXXX * XXXX XXXX XXXX posted XX/XX/XXXX {$35.00} fee applied XX/XX/XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XX/XX/XXXX {$35.00} fee applied XX/XX/XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXXXXXX XXXX XXXX posted XX/XX/XXXX {$35.00} fee applied XX/XX/XXXX XXXX XXXX NJ posted XX/XX/XXXX {$35.00} fee applied XX/XX/XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX posted XX/XX/XXXX {$35.00} fee applied XX/XX/XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXXXXXX XXXX posted XX/XX/XXXX {$35.00} fee applied XX/XX/XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX posted XX/XX/XXXX {$35.00} fee applied XX/XX/XXXX XXXX XXXX  XXXX XXXX  posted XX/XX/XXXX {$35.00} fee applied XX/XX/XXXX That is {$420.00} in overdraft fees identified by Wells Fargo . Wells Fargo also says one {$35.00} courtesy refund was issued on XX/XX/XXXX for the XX/XX/XXXX XXXX transaction, which means at least {$380.00} remains outstanding even under Wells Fargos own accounting. 

These fees should not have been dismissed with a blanket statement about Wells Fargos posting practices. The bank still must explain, transaction by transaction and fee by fee, why each overdraft fee remains valid, especially where the fees were tied to disputed transactions and where Wells Fargo reversed provisional credit after denying the claim. The CFPBs official commentary states that if an error occurred, the institution must correct the error, including refunding fees imposed by the institution where applicable, and that upon debiting provisional credit, the institution may not impose fees for items it is required to honor under 1005.11.

Wells Fargos letter also states that it reversed the provisional credit of {$74.00} on XX/XX/XXXX. If Wells Fargo debited provisional credit after concluding no error occurred, Regulation E required a written explanation of findings and notice of my right to request the documents relied upon, and those documents must be provided promptly upon request. 12 C.F.R. 1005.11 ( d ) ( 1 ) - ( 2 ) ; 15 U.S.C. 1693f ( d ).

Wells Fargos handling of this matter also appears to raise UDAAP concerns under the Consumer Financial Protection Act. The CFPB may prevent a covered person from engaging in an unfair, deceptive, or abusive act or practice in connection with a consumer financial product or service, and it is unlawful for a covered person to engage in an unfair, deceptive, or abusive act or practice. 12 U.S.C. 5531 ( a ), 5536 ( a ) ( 1 ) ( B ). An act or practice is unfair where it causes or is likely to cause substantial injury to consumers, the injury is not reasonably avoidable by consumers, and the injury is not outweighed by countervailing benefits to consumers or competition. 12 U.S.C. 5531 ( c ). Here, Wells Fargo reversed provisional credit, retained overdraft fees, and provided only a conclusory explanation rather than a transparent, evidence-based explanation of how it determined the disputed transactions were authorized. That caused concrete monetary harm to me in the form of unreimbursed fees and charges, and that harm was not reasonably avoidable once the bank controlled the investigation, the provisional credit decision, and the fee determinations. To the extent Wells Fargo failed to conduct a sufficient investigation, failed to provide a meaningful explanation, or retained fees without adequate support, that conduct appears to raise unfairness concerns under UDAAP.

I am asking CFPB to require Wells Fargo to : 1. Reopen the investigation.

2. Produce all documents and evidence relied upon in denying claim number XXXX and reversing the provisional credit. 
3. Provide a transaction-by-transaction explanation of how Wells Fargo concluded the disputed activity was authorized.

4. Provide a fee-by-fee explanation of why each of the 12 overdraft fees remains valid. 
5. Refund all overdraft fees identified in the letter, totaling {$420.00}, or at minimum refund the outstanding {$380.00} Wells Fargo still appears to owe after the single {$35.00} courtesy refund it claims to have issued. 
6. Confirm whether Wells Fargo fully complied with the timing, investigation, provisional credit, written explanation, and document-production requirements of the Electronic Fund Transfer Act and Regulation E.

7. Evaluate whether Wells Fargos conduct also constitutes an unfair act or practice under the Consumer Financial Protection Act/UDAAP provisions. 

My position is straightforward : Wells Fargo has not shown a sufficient basis to deny my claim, has not provided the records it relied upon, and has not justified retaining the overdraft fees listed in its own response. I request that CFPB continue to pursue this matter and require a substantive response with supporting documentation.

text:
XX/XX/year> My initial complaint with the CFPB was dated XX/XX/year> regarding changes made by Wells Fargo to small business accounts, including raising the minimum balance requirement from {$500.00} to {$2000.00} and increasing the monthly fee to {$15.00} ( previously {$10.00} ), even if the account drops below that threshold for a single day. 

Please reference Case # : XXXX. 

I am still waiting for a clear explanation of something that, frankly, does not make sense to me as a small business owner : How do you justify increasing the minimum balance requirement from {$500.00} to {$2000.00} on small business accounts?

That is a 300 % increase in required idle capital just to avoid fees. For many small businesses, that is not a minor adjustmentthat is money that is actively needed to operate the business. And the part that makes even less sense is this : If a business dips below that threshold for even a short period, the full monthly fee is still charged. That means normal cash flow activitypaying monthly expenses, paying
vendors, seasonal fluctuationscan trigger penalties.

This is not banking support. It is a system designed to profit from normal volatility in small business cash flow.

To understand the scale of this, using publicly available figures that Wells Fargo serves millions of small business customers, even a conservative illustration shows the impact : If millions of accounts are now required to maintain {$2000.00} instead of {$500.00}, that represents billions of dollars ( my conservative calculations estimate it to be {$9.00} XXXX -- but, please, correct me if I'm way off ) in small business working capital held idle to avoid fees.

And if even a portion of those accounts fall below the threshold in a given month, the resulting fees scale into tens of millions of dollars monthly. Again, annually, we're talking over {$400.00} XXXX. 
This is not theoretical. It is simple math applied across a national customer base.

So my questions remain very direct : Why was {$2000.00} chosen as the new minimum for small business accounts?

What benefit does that level provide to small business customersnot the bank?

Why is a temporary balance dip treated as justification for a full monthly fee?

How is this structure consistent with supporting small business stability?

From where I sit, this policy forces small businesses to keep significantly more money idle just to avoid fees. For me, Im not able to use {$10000.00} out of my business accounts. On top of that, being penalized when I use that money in the normal course of operating my business.

I am requesting a clear, direct explanation of the reasoning behind this structure. I do not need a reiteration of policy and compliance as seen in the attached letter.

On XX/XX/XXXX, Klarna mailed a collection letter to my home demanding {$85.00} for the first fraudulent account. When I contacted Klarna customer service, their representative confirmed that the account was registered under an XXXX email address that does not belong to me direct evidence that someone else opened the account. Despite this, Klarna continued collection activity. On XX/XX/XXXX, Klarna sent XXXX additional collection letters XXXX escalating the first account to {$130.00}, and XXXX revealing a second fraudulent account demanding {$120.00}. Total fraudulent debt : approximately {$980.00}. 

I have taken the following actions : ( XXXX ) Filed an FTC Identity Theft Report at XXXX ; ( XXXX ) Filed a police report with the XXXX XXXX XXXX ( Case # XXXX ) ; ( XXXX ) Sent Klarna a formal identity theft dispute letter via USPS certified mail demanding account closure, cessation of collection, and written confirmation of non-liability ; ( XXXX ) Filed disputes with XXXX XXXX, and XXXX ; ( XXXX ) Filed a complaint with the Texas Attorney General. 

This is part of a well-documented fraud pattern known as the " Klarna Method, '' where criminals use stolen identity data to exploit Klarna 's minimal identity verification and open fraudulent BNPL accounts XXXX Klarna acknowledged this fraud wave publicly on XX/XX/XXXX. text: On Tuesday XX/XX/XXXX, I was the victim of a bank impersonation scam. The caller ID displayed Chase so I answered the call. The man stated there was suspicious activity on my account and that a checking account was opened in XXXX XXXX, XXXX  by a person named XXXX XXXX who made XXXX large XXXX payments from that account in the amounts of {$2000.00} and {$3000.00}. He provided me with the transaction numbers. I reasonably believed I was speaking to Chases fraud department. Under false pretenses, I was instructed to send multiple XXXX payments in order to secure my account. The impersonating representative told me that if I coded the transaction by typing three zeros before the additional numbers he provided that it would code the transaction in a way that would not withdraw any funds from my account. He did this several times and a couple of the XXXX transactions were canceled by Chase Bank. After I got off the phone with this impersonating representative, I immediately called Chase Bank customer service to unfreeze my account and confirm that no money had been withdrawn from my checking account. The woman I spoke to said that it was a scam. After speaking with that representative for approximately 13 minutes, I immediately called the Chase Bank fraud department speaking to a different representative explaining the impersonation scam I just fell victim too. On Friday XX/XX/XXXX, I received and email from Chase saying a letter was just sent to me updating me my claim has been denied reimbursement despite clear evidence of spoofed impersonation. Chase denied my claim stating the transaction was authorized. While I did press send, I did so under deception by an individual impersonating Chase Bank using a spoofed Chase phone number. I reasonably believed I was securing my funds from fraud. This was not a voluntary payment, it was induced under false pretenses. I reported the claim in a timely fashion that same day within minutes of getting off the phone with the impersonation scammers. Today, XX/XX/XXXX I called Chase Bank claims department to discuss the reasoning behind my denial. The initial woman I spoke to told me the funds were unable to be retrieved, and that was the reason for the denial. As I questioned and discussed further with her at the call got disconnected and she never returned my phone call. I called back and got a completely different chase bank claims department representative. He gave me a completely different reason for the denial which was that there was another transaction made to the same number within 120 days so therefore there was no impersonation and it wasnt authorized transaction. I told that representative I would like to file a formal appeal or be transferred to a different person or department that could escalate my appeal further and he repeatedly told me there was nothing else Chase Bank could do to help in the matter.
label: fraud_scam
source_product: Money transfer, virtual currency, or money service

text:
On XX/XX/year>, I discovered that XXXX XXXX XXXX reported a debt in the amount of {$24000.00} on my credit report. I do not recognize this debt and did not receive prior written notice or proper validation before it was reported. 
On XX/XX/year>, I contacted XXXX XXXX XXXX and formally requested validation of the alleged {$24000.00} debt pursuant to the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act ( FDCPA ), 15 U.S.C. 1692g. I specifically requested : The name and address of the original creditor A complete payment history Documentation proving I am legally obligated for this debt Proof that XXXX XXXX XXXX has the legal authority to collect this debt On XX/XX/year>, I filed disputes with the credit reporting agencies under the Fair Credit Reporting Act ( FCRA ), 15 U.S.C. 1681i, challenging the accuracy and completeness of this account. 
On XX/XX/year>, I followed up with XXXX XXXX XXXX because I had not received sufficient documentation. As of XX/XX/year>, I have not received proper validation of this debt. Despite my disputes, the account continues to be reported on my credit file. 
Under the FDCPA, a debt collector must cease collection activities until validation is provided. Additionally, under the FCRA, information furnished to credit reporting agencies must be accurate and verifiable. Reporting an unverified debt may constitute a violation of federal law. 
I am requesting that XXXX XXXX XXXX : Provide full and proper validation of the {$24000.00} debt ; or Immediately delete this account from all credit reporting agencies if they can not verify its accuracy. 
If this matter is not resolved promptly, I am prepared to pursue further action to protect my rights under federal law. A fair resolution to this issue would include the following : 1. XXXX XXXX XXXX Account {$24000.00} Regarding the {$24000.00} account reported by XXXX XXXX XXXX, I am requesting : Full validation of the debt pursuant to the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act ( FDCPA ), including : Name and address of the original creditor A signed agreement or contract bearing my signature A complete payment history Documentation showing legal assignment and authority to collect If this documentation can not be provided, I am requesting : Immediate deletion of the account from all credit reporting agencies Written confirmation that collection activity has ceased Confirmation that the debt will not be sold or transferred 2. XXXX XXXX University Student Loans Regarding the student loans associated with XXXX XXXX University, I am requesting : Written verification of the original loan amounts and disbursement dates A full accounting of the current balance, including interest and fees Complete loan servicer history Verification that all reporting complies with the Fair Credit Reporting Act ( FCRA ) If these loans are federal student loans, I am also requesting review under : Borrower Defense to Repayment provisions if applicable Any applicable Department of Education discharge, forgiveness, or settlement programs Confirmation that the loans were properly certified and that institutional representations were accurate at the time of enrollment If inaccurate reporting, improper servicing, or misrepresentation occurred, I am requesting : Immediate correction or deletion of inaccurate reporting Review for potential discharge, adjustment, or relief Written confirmation of any corrective action taken My goal is to ensure my credit report reflects only accurate and legally verifiable information and that I receive any protections or relief available under federal law.

text:
I am filing this complaint regarding a fraud claim that was denied by Navy Federal Credit Union. I was the victim of a sophisticated impersonation scam in which individuals spoofed Navy Federals official customer service phone number and falsely represented themselves as Navy Federal employees. 
On XX/XX/year>, I received a phone call that appeared on my phone as coming from Navy Federal Credit Unions official customer service number ( XXXX ). Because the caller ID displayed the legitimate Navy Federal number and my phone even labeled it as Navy Federal CU, I reasonably believed I was speaking directly with my financial institution. 
During the call, the individuals claimed there was suspicious activity involving my account and instructed me to take steps to secure the account. They used verification-style procedures that made the interaction appear legitimate and consistent with a normal fraud prevention call from a bank. 
Following their instructions, I was directed to send funds through XXXX XXXX as part of what I believed was a process to correct or reverse fraudulent activity on my account. At the time, I genuinely believed I was following instructions from Navy Federal Credit Union in order to protect my account. 
My debit card was never lost or stolen, and I did not knowingly authorize a payment to a scammer. The transaction occurred only because I was manipulated by individuals impersonating Navy Federal employees through caller ID spoofing.

I have attached screenshots of my phone call log showing the incoming call from Navy Federal Credit Unions official number, which demonstrates how the scammers impersonated the bank and gained my trust. 
I reported the incident and filed a fraud claim with Navy Federal Credit Union as soon as I realized I had been deceived. However, my claim and subsequent appeal were denied on the basis that the transaction was considered authorized. 
I respectfully disagree with this determination because my authorization was obtained through deception and impersonation of my financial institution. Had I known I was not communicating with Navy Federal Credit Union, I would never have initiated the transaction. 
I am requesting that Navy Federal Credit Union review this matter again and reconsider my fraud claim, as the transaction occurred as a direct result of a bank impersonation scam.
label: fraud_scam
source_product: Money transfer, virtual currency, or money service

text:
On XX/XX/year>, I sent a XXXX payment in the amount of {$220.00}. At the time of the transaction, the recipient name displayed in the XXXX system appeared as a business entity : XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. Based on this information, I reasonably believed that I was sending payment to a legitimate business. 



However, after the transaction was completed, I later learned that the funds were not received by the intended business. Instead, the payment was directed to a different individual or account that is not associated with the business name shown during the transaction. 



I immediately contacted Bank of America and filed a claim. I explained that I relied on the recipient name displayed in the XXXX system, which was misleading and caused me to reasonably believe I was paying the correct business. Bank of America denied my claim, stating that the transaction was authorized. 



I then requested a re-evaluation of the claim. Despite providing the same explanation, the bank upheld their original decision and did not provide a clear explanation as to why the recipient name displayed did not match the actual recipient of the funds. 



Additionally, I attempted to contact the recipient directly multiple times, but received no response. 



This situation is not simply a user error. The transaction was made based on misleading recipient information presented within the XXXX system. As a consumer, I relied on the name shown during the transaction, and I believe there was a lack of adequate safeguards to prevent this type of misleading payment scenario. 



I am requesting reimbursement of the {$220.00} and a proper investigation into how the recipient name displayed in the XXXX system did not match the actual recipient of the funds.

On the XXXX, XXXX unauthorized XXXX transactions totaling {$3500.00} ( {$3000.00} and {$500.00} ) were sent from my account. I did not authorize these transactions. 
In addition, there were XXXX earlier unauthorized XXXX transactions to the same individual in the amounts of {$20.00} ( XXXX ) and {$80.00} ( early XXXX ). At the time, I was unaware that these transactions were part of fraudulent activity. I now understand that these transactions were also unauthorized and part of a scam involving a person I do not know. 
This brings the total amount of unauthorized transactions to {$3500.00}. 
I reported the fraud immediately in person at a PNC Bank branch on the XXXX. A bank teller assisted me and notified the fraud department at that time. 
I was later informed that my claim had been denied. I was not contacted directly by the fraud department, did not receive a written denial letter, and was not provided a detailed explanation of how this decision was made. 
The only justification provided was that I had previously sent XXXX small XXXX payments ( {$20.00} and {$80.00} ) to the same individual. I have since clarified that I do not know this individual and that these transactions were also unauthorized and part of fraudulent activity. 
Additionally, during a follow-up discussion, I was informed that the disputed {$3500.00} transactions were not sent from my mobile device. Despite this critical information, the claim was still denied without a reasonable explanation or further investigation. 
This raises serious concerns that PNC Bank did not conduct a reasonable or thorough investigation into whether my account was compromised. 
PNC Bank has not provided any documentation demonstrating that it reviewed essential information such as : * Device used to initiate the transactions * IP address or geographic location * Login or access history * Authentication methods used to approve the transactions Instead, the denial appears to be based on incorrect assumptions and incomplete information. 
Additionally, I was not provided provisional credit for the disputed amount while this matter was under review. 
Under the Electronic Fund Transfer Act ( Regulation XXXX ), financial institutions are required to conduct a reasonable investigation into unauthorized electronic transactions, provide written explanations of their findings, and issue provisional credit if the investigation is not completed within required timeframes. 
I am also concerned that my access to mobile banking was restricted without notice during this process, which limited my ability to monitor my account and respond appropriately.

text:
I am requesting the CFPBs assistance in determining whether Capital One continued contacting me or receiving creditrelated information after they reported an account under my personal information as charged off. I am seeking clarification on whether Capital One continued accessing or monitoring my credit information after the reported chargeoff date, and if so, what the legal basis was for doing so. 

I am not making any assumptions. I am asking Capital One to confirm or deny specific activity and provide documentation. 

Details of the Complaint Capital One reported an account under my personal information as charged off ; I am seeking clarification regarding their continued communications afterward. My understanding is that once an account is reported as charged off, transferred, or sold, the creditor no longer maintains an active customer relationship and therefore no longer has a permissible purpose under the Fair Credit Reporting Act ( FCRA ) to access or monitor a consumers credit file unless a new, legally valid purpose exists. 

Despite this, I continued receiving communications from Capital Ones CreditWise  service after the reported chargeoff date. These communications appeared to reference changes to my credit information, including alerts related to my XXXX XXXX XXXX  credit reports. 

I am not making any assumptions about Capital Ones actions. I am requesting clarification and documentation so I can understand whether Capital One continued contacting me or receiving creditrelated information after the account was reported as charged off, and if so, what the legal basis was for doing so. 

Information Requested From Capital One I respectfully request that Capital One answer the following : 1. Did Capital One continue sending emails or alerts to me after the account was reported as charged off? 
If so, please provide : the dates the times the subject lines and the purpose of each communication I am not requesting the content of the emails only confirmation of whether they were sent.

2. Did Capital One continue receiving creditrelated alerts or data from XXXX XXXX XXXX after the reported chargeoff date? 
If so, please explain : what information was received why it was received and under what permissible purpose 3. Was Capital One aware that once an account is reported as charged off or sold, the creditor no longer has a permissible purpose to access a consumers credit file? 
I am requesting confirmation of their understanding of this requirement.

4. If Capital One continued contacting me after the reported chargeoff date, what was the legal basis for doing so?

I am requesting a clear explanation of the permissible purpose relied upon.

5. If Capital One did not continue contacting me, then what explains the communications referencing changes to my credit information? 
I am requesting clarification on how these communications were generated. 

Why CFPB Assistance Is Needed I can not determine : whether Capital One accessed my credit file whether they received alerts from any credit bureau whether they continued monitoring my credit information whether they had a permissible purpose whether they were aware of the chargeoff status I am requesting the CFPBs assistance in obtaining clear answers and documentation. text:
I am submitting this explanation to dispute the response provided by SoFi regarding unauthorized activity on my account. 

On XX/XX/2026, I reported that my mobile phone had been stolen during an incident in which I was drugged and incapacitated. Because my phone contained my financial applications and authentication methods, the perpetrator had physical access to my device while I was unable to secure it. 

Shortly after this incident, a SoFi Crypto account was opened in my name without my knowledge or authorization, and {$9500.00} was transferred from my SoFi Savings Account into that crypto account and converted into XXXX. 

I immediately reported this activity to SoFi on XX/XX/2026. SoFi conducted an investigation and concluded on XX/XX/2026 that no unauthorized access had occurred. This determination was based primarily on login history indicating that my device continued to access the account using biometric authentication. 

However, the login history provided by SoFi contradicts this conclusion. The data shows that on XX/XX/2026, my account was accessed from multiple geographic locations within a short period of time, including XXXX, XXXX ( where the theft occurred ), as well as XXXX, XXXX and other U.S. locations. These access points are not physically possible for a single user and indicate unauthorized access. 

Additionally, these login events occurred during the timeframe in which I was incapacitated. The login history also shows numerous failed login attempts in the days following the incident from multiple locations, which is consistent with unauthorized attempts to gain or maintain access to my account. 

SoFis conclusion relies solely on the fact that my device was used. However, because my device was stolen and compromised, the use of that device does not establish that I authorized the transactions. Instead, the evidence supports a device compromise and account takeover. 

There are also inconsistencies in SoFis investigation process. Although they state the investigation was completed on XX/XX/2026, I was informed on XX/XX/2026 that the claim was still under review. Additionally, SoFi acknowledged that no restriction was placed on the crypto account during the investigation, allowing continued access to the compromised account. 

Under the * * Electronic Fund Transfer Act * * and * * Regulation E * *, an electronic fund transfer is considered unauthorized if it is initiated by a person other than the consumer without actual authority, even if the device or authentication method was used. 

Given the documented evidence of account takeover, I believe SoFi failed to conduct a reasonable investigation and incorrectly determined that the activity was authorized.

I respectfully request that the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau review this matter and require SoFi to reevaluate the claim based on the full evidence, including the login history demonstrating unauthorized access.
label: fraud_scam

I am filing this complaint because I believe Wells Fargo failed to adequately protect me as a consumer in the following ways : XXXX. 1. Lack of Consumer Protection and Recourse : I reasonably believed that my bank would provide some level of protection against fraud, especially for a transaction of this magnitude. I was not informed that I would bear the full loss in the event of fraud.

text:
I am filing a complaint regarding unauthorized activity on my Coinbase account and Coinbases improper attempt to hold me liable for resulting losses and transactions. 

On XX/XX/year>, unauthorized activity occurred on my Coinbase account involving rapid buy/sell transactions and liquidation of my crypto assets, resulting in losses of approximately {$7800.00}. 

I did not discover this activity until XX/XX/year>, when I received notice from my bank that my account was overdrawn due to an unauthorized {$6000.00} transfer to Coinbase. After seeing this alert, I immediately logged into my bank account and confirmed I had not authorized the transfer. I then logged into my Coinbase account and discovered the unauthorized activity.

Coinbase denied my claim and asserted that the activity was authorized. However, Coinbases findings contain multiple factual inconsistencies : Coinbase states the account access occurred on XX/XX/year>, while the transactions they provided all occurred on XX/XX/year>. 
Coinbase claims my password was reset, 2FA was used, and a new device was confirmed via email. However : I never received any password reset email I never received any 2FA code I never received any new device confirmation email My password remained unchanged and still worked when I logged in on XX/XX/XXXX Coinbase is also attempting to hold me liable for approximately {$6000.00} tied to the unauthorized bank transfer, even though : The transfer was unauthorized The funds had not yet cleared I stopped the transfer through my bank Coinbase nevertheless allowed trading against the uncleared funds Additionally, Coinbase sent conflicting communications regarding this liability. One communication stated that I owed the {$6000.00}, while a subsequent investigation email made no mention of the liability or how it was determined.

I have requested supporting authentication logs and records from Coinbase, including password reset logs, 2FA delivery records, and device access information, but the investigation conclusions provided to me do not match the actual sequence of events or the records available to me.

I am requesting : A proper and accurate investigation Reimbursement of the unauthorized losses Removal of the disputed {$6000.00} liability A detailed explanation reconciling the inconsistencies in Coinbases findings I have preserved documentation supporting these facts and communications.
label: fraud_scam
source_product: Money transfer, virtual currency, or money service

text:
CFPB COMPLAINT PAYPAL ( SECOND COMPLAINT / ESCALATION ) Company : PayPal , Inc . 
Issue : Unfair dispute handling, improper account restriction, UDAAP violations, failure to honor Seller Protection, financial injury Product : Payment services What happened : I am filing this complaint because PayPal has continued to mishandle a {$10000.00} transaction dispute, imposed improper fees, and permanently restricted my account without justification. PayPals response to my initial CFPB complaint was incomplete and failed to address the core issues.

Transaction Background In XX/XX/year>, I sold {$10000.00} worth of electrical equipment. The buyer paid through PayPal. I complied with every Seller Protection requirement, including : Shipping to the buyers confirmed address Providing valid UPS tracking Delivery confirmation Adult signature confirmation Photographs of the shipment and labels A fully paid invoice Buyer communication screenshots Despite this, the buyer falsely claimed Item Not Received. I submitted all evidence proving delivery and signature confirmation. 

PayPal Wrongfully Refunded the Buyer PayPal refunded the buyer in full, removed {$10000.00} from my account, and left me without the merchandise or the funds. PayPal has not identified any valid exception to Seller Protection. 

Improper Fees After reversing the transaction, PayPal attempted to charge me over {$400.00} in additional fees, even though the dispute was caused by buyer fraud and I fully complied with PayPals policies.

Permanent Account Restriction PayPal then permanently restricted my account, stating I could no longer use PayPal or receive Seller Protection. No explanation was provided for : Why Seller Protection was denied Why the account was restricted Why additional fees were imposed Why clear evidence of delivery was ignored Failure to Address Issues in Their CFPB Response PayPals response to my first CFPB complaint did not address : Why signatureconfirmed delivery was disregarded Why Seller Protection was denied Why I was charged additional fees Why my account was permanently restricted Why the buyer was refunded despite clear evidence of fraud Why no written explanation was provided Their response was generic, incomplete, and avoided the core violations. 



Why this is a regulatory issue : PayPals actions constitute unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices ( UDAAP ) because : They ignored clear evidence required under their own Seller Protection policy They enabled buyer fraud They imposed improper fees They permanently restricted my account without cause They failed to provide a meaningful explanation or justification They caused a financial loss exceeding {$10000.00} This is not a customer service issuethis is a systemic failure in PayPals dispute handling and compliance process. 

Subject : PayPals Response Contains Material Misrepresentations, Procedural Violations, and Clear UDAAP Violations I am submitting this rebuttal because PayPals response is factually incorrect, internally contradictory, and openly admits to conduct that violates both the PayPal User Agreement and federal consumer protection law.

PayPals own written response contains multiple fatal admissions that prove the {$10000.00} refund was issued improperly, without evidence review, and solely due to an internal account limitation unrelated to the transaction.

Below is a structured breakdown of PayPals violations.

1. PayPal admits I provided all required documentation on XX/XX/year> PayPal states I provided : Invoices Transactional information Photos of the items Proof of ID This means PayPal had everything required to evaluate the transaction.

Yet they admit they did not evaluate any of it.

2. PayPal admits the buyer filed the claim AFTER PayPal limited my account Their timeline shows : XX/XX/XXXX : PayPal limits account XX/XX/XXXX : I provide all requested documentation XX/XX/XXXX : Buyer files Item Not Received claim XX/XX/XXXX : PayPal immediately closes claim in buyers favor This is the key issue : PayPal refunded the buyer because my account was limited, not because of evidence. 

This is a direct violation of : PayPal Seller Protection Policy PayPal User Agreement CFPB UDAAP standards FTC unfair business practice rules Seller Protection eligibility is based on transaction compliance, not account status. 


3. PayPal admits they never reviewed tracking, delivery, or signature PayPals response contains XXXX reference to : Tracking number Delivery confirmation Adult signature Address match Carrier proof Because they never reviewed it.

Instead, they state : We closed the claim in the buyers favor because your account was permanently limited.

This is an admission that the refund was procedurally automatic, not evidencebased.

This is a textbook unfair and deceptive practice.

4. PayPal falsely claims customers were dissatisfied PayPal states : It appears customers were dissatisfied with this businesss service.

This is a false statement.

PayPal provided : No complaints No disputes No chargebacks No negative feedback No evidence of dissatisfaction This is a material misrepresentation used to justify an improper limitation.

5. PayPal admits they denied both appeals for risk, not evidence PayPal states both appeals were denied due to the reasons above, which are : Account risk Internal review Not transaction evidence This is another admission that : PayPal never evaluated the transaction on its merits.

This violates their own Seller Protection rules.

6. PayPal admits they charged a dispute fee for a dispute they refused to adjudicate They charged a {$30.00} dispute fee even though : They did not allow me to defend the claim They did not review evidence They closed the case automatically Charging a fee for a process they refused to provide is another UDAAP violation.

7. XXXX final justification is legally invalid PayPal states : We are unable to extend protection because your account is not in good standing.

This is the most serious admission.

Seller Protection eligibility is based on : Proof of shipment Proof of delivery Signature Address match NOT account standing.

This is a direct violation of PayPals own contract and a deceptive business practice.

8. What I am requesting I am requesting : 1. Immediate reinstatement of the {$10000.00} improperly refunded to the buyer 2. Removal of all dispute fees and negative balance charges 3. Correction of PayPals false statements regarding dissatisfied customers 4. A full investigation into PayPals procedural misconduct 5. A review by PayPals Executive Escalations team, not automated risk staff PayPals own response proves : They did not follow their own policies They did not evaluate evidence They refunded a buyer solely due to an internal limitation They misrepresented facts to justify their actions 9. Closing Statement PayPals response is not only inadequate it is selfincriminating.

Their own admissions confirm that the refund was improper, the appeals were mishandled, and the denial of Seller Protection was unlawful. 

I request immediate CFPB intervention. 


What resolution I am requesting : I am requesting the CFPBs assistance to require PayPal to : 1. Reinstate the {$10000.00} removed from my account 2. Remove all improper fees charged in connection with this dispute 3. Provide a written explanation for : Denial of Seller Protection Permanent account restriction Ignoring signatureconfirmed delivery 4. Correct procedural and compliance failures in their dispute handling 5. Restore my account access or provide a legally valid reason for the restriction PayPals actions have caused significant financial harm and enabled clear buyer fraud. Their failure to address the issues in their initial CFPB response requires escalation
label: fraud_scam
source_product: Money transfer, virtual currency, or money service

text:
Account was opened XX/XX/year> Amount owed {$1000.00} XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX  Portfolio recovery I am formally disputing the alleged debt you continue reporting on my consumer credit file regarding the above-referenced account allegedly associated with XXXX. 

This account has previously been disputed multiple times, yet your company continues reporting this collection account without providing competent evidence that this debt is accurate, valid, legally collectible, or belongs to me. Your failure to provide sufficient documentation while continuing to report derogatory information may constitute violations of the Fair Credit Reporting Act ( FCRA ) and the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act ( FDCPA ).

You are hereby required to provide complete validation and verification of this alleged debt, including but not limited to the following : 1. A complete copy of the original signed contract or application bearing my signature.

2. Full payment history and account statements from the original creditor showing how the alleged balance was calculated.

3. Documentation showing how your company obtained authority to collect this alleged debt.

4. Complete chain of assignment and transfer records from the original creditor to your company.

5. A complete bill of sale specifically identifying this account and alleged transfer.

6. Proof that your company is properly licensed and authorized to collect debts in my state.

Additionally, provide : * The original date of delinquency.

* The name and address of the original creditor.

* An itemized accounting of the alleged balance.

* Any agreements bearing my signature.

* Any records of communication allegedly connected to this account.

Be advised that generic account summaries, spreadsheets, screenshots, or computer-generated documents created by your company will not constitute proper validation of this debt.

Until you provide the requested documentation, you must cease reporting this account as verified or accurate to any consumer reporting agency. If you can not provide competent evidence that this debt belongs to me and that your reporting is fully compliant with federal law, you must immediately delete this account from all consumer credit reporting agencies.

Failure to comply may result in complaints being filed with the : * Consumer Financial Protection Bureau * Federal Trade Commission * My State Attorney General Any continued reporting of inaccurate, unverified, or misleading information after receipt of this notice may be considered willful noncompliance under the FCRA and FDCPA.

I demand a full investigation and written response within the time permitted by law.
label: fraud_scam

text:
I am submitting a complaint against several debt collection agencies for what appear to be ongoing violations of federal consumer protection laws. After formally disputing the alleged debts and requesting proper validation, the agencies failed to provide sufficient documentation to establish that I am legally responsible for these accounts. Specifically, they did not produce key records such as original agreements, proof of ownership, complete account histories, or evidence of their authority to collect. 

Despite this lack of verification, collection activities and credit reporting appear to have continued. This raises concerns about potential violations of the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act ( FDCPA ), including failure to properly validate debts and the use of misleading or unfair collection practices. It also raises concerns under the Fair Credit Reporting Act ( FCRA ), particularly with respect to the accuracy of reported information and the adequacy of any investigation into disputed accounts. 

Overall, the conduct described suggests that these agencies may be attempting to collect on unverified debts and may be reporting potentially inaccurate information, indicating possible broader noncompliance with federal consumer protection requirements.
label: fraud_scam

text:
Request for Debt Validation & Dispute of Account Creditor/Collection Agency : Midland Credit Management Debt : {$1200.00} To Whom It May Concern, I am writing in response to your claim regarding the above-referenced account, which I previously disputed. You indicated that my prior dispute was unsubstantiated. I am formally disputing this debt again and requesting full validation as is my right under the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act ( FDCPA ), 15 U.S.C. 1692g.

I do not acknowledge this debt as valid and request that you provide competent evidence that I have a legal obligation to pay. Specifically, please provide the following : A copy of the original signed contract or agreement bearing my signature Documentation showing how the amount owed was calculated The name and address of the original creditor Proof that you are authorized to collect on this debt ( including assignment or transfer documentation ) A complete payment history on this account Verification that this debt is within the applicable statute of limitations Additionally, if you have reported this account to any credit reporting agency, I request that you mark the account as disputed immediately.

If you are unable to provide the requested documentation, you must cease collection efforts and remove this account from my credit report in accordance with federal law.

Please note that I am requesting all future communications regarding this matter be in writing.

Thank you for your prompt attention to this matter.

text:
I am submitting this complaint regarding my XXXX XXXX account serviced by Synchrony Bank. 

During a period of financial hardship, I fell behind on my payments after experiencing significant financial difficulties. Despite my efforts to manage the account and communicate responsibly, XXXX reported multiple late payments that severely damaged my XXXX XXXX. I had some issues with possible fraudulent charges but they would either disconnect me from the phone or simply hang up on me when I questioned charges. 

I am requesting a full review of the account history, payment reporting, and any hardship assistance options that may have been available during this period. I believe the reporting and handling of the account did not properly reflect the circumstances surrounding my financial hardship. 

These negative marks are now significantly impacting my ability to rebuild my financial stability and pursue important opportunities, including business financing and credit recovery. Since this hardship period, I have been actively working to improve my financial situation and maintain better payment habits. 

I respectfully request that Synchrony Bank consider a goodwill adjustment and remove the late payment reporting associated with this hardship period. At minimum, I am requesting a detailed investigation into the accuracy and handling of the reporting on this account. 

I appreciate your time and consideration and hope this matter can be resolved fairly.

text:
I am submitting this complaint regarding fraudulent and inaccurate information reporting on my consumer credit reports. I am a victim of identity theft and did not authorize several collection accounts and hard inquiries currently appearing on my credit file.

I previously disputed this information directly with the credit reporting agencies ; however, the accounts and inquiries continue reporting without proper verification or proof of permissible purpose.

The information being reported is inaccurate, incomplete, misleading, and fraudulent. I am disputing the validity, ownership, and accuracy of the collections and inquiries associated with my credit reports.

Under 15 U.S.C. 1681e ( b ), credit reporting agencies are required to assure maximum possible accuracy of the information they report. Under 15 U.S.C. 1681i, they are required to conduct a reasonable reinvestigation of disputed information. Additionally, under 15 U.S.C. 1681b, companies must have a permissible purpose before accessing a consumer credit report.

Despite my prior disputes, the inaccurate and fraudulent information remains on my reports.
label: fraud_scam

source_sub_issue: Debt was result of identity theft

text: I am submitting this formal complaint to the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau ( CFPB ) to report a fraudulent debt collection attempt involving identity theft. A lawsuit has been filed against me by TENAGLIA & HUNT, P.A., representing XXXX XXXX XXXX, the assignee of a debt allegedly owed to XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXXXXXX, in the amount of {$3300.00} and/or any other amount. I am not the debtor in this case. I have never entered into any contract or financial obligation with XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXXXXXX, nor have I incurred any debt to them. This lawsuit is based on fraudulent activity where my personal information was unlawfully used by an identity thief without my consent. I did not authorize any of the transactions or account openings associated with this alleged debt. The legal action being pursued against me is an unjust attempt to collect a debt that does not belong to me, and I have never received any goods, services, or benefits from the transactions made under this fraudulent account. As a victim of identity theft, I should not be held liable for debts that are not mine. This situation has caused me significant emotional distress as I am being unjustly accused and harassed for a debt created through the wrongful use of my personal information. The actions of XXXX XXXX XXXX, and TENAGLIA & HUNT, P.A. violate several consumer protection laws. The fraudulent claim is a violation of the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act ( FDCPA ), 15 U.S.C. 1692, which requires debt collectors to validate the debt before pursuing legal action. XXXX XXXX, XXXX, and their representatives have failed to validate the debt and continue to pursue a lawsuit based on fraudulent information. Additionally, the Fair Credit Reporting Act ( FCRA ), 15 U.S.C. 1681, provides protections regarding the accuracy of credit information. XXXX XXXX and TENAGLIA & HUNT have failed to ensure the legitimacy of the debt and have not properly investigated its origin, which violates my rights under the FCRA. Furthermore, under the Fair and Accurate Credit Transactions Act ( FACTA ), 15 U.S.C. 1681c-2, victims of identity theft can challenge fraudulent accounts and require creditors to remove them. XXXX XXXX and TENAGLIA & HUNT have continued pursuing this fraudulent debt, disregarding FACTA protections. Moreover, this situation involves fraudulent misrepresentation. TENAGLIA & HUNT and XXXX XXXX  are knowingly attempting to collect a debt that I do not owe. They have failed to investigate the legitimacy of the debt, and their pursuit constitutes fraud. Their actions have resulted in unjust enrichment, unlawfully attempting to benefit from a debt that is not mine. The emotional toll this situation has caused has been severe. The constant worry about my financial future and the distress of being wrongfully pursued have negatively impacted my mental and emotional well-being. This situation has caused significant XXXX, frustration, and helplessness. I now struggle with sleepless nights while trying to clear my name. I am submitting this complaint to the CFPB to report these fraudulent actions and to request immediate intervention. I ask that the CFPB take appropriate action to ensure that XXXX XXXX  and TENAGLIA & HUNT cease their illegal collection efforts, protect my rights as a victim of identity theft, and hold those responsible accountable under the law. I respectfully request the immediate dismissal of this case.

text:
After receiving transaction records from Cash App, I learned that unauthorized Bitcoin and cryptocurrency-related transactions had been occurring on my account as early as XX/XX/year>, without my knowledge. I was not aware of these earlier transactions until Cash App later provided transaction history during the dispute process. 

On XX/XX/year>, XXXXXXXX XXXX contacted me regarding suspicious transactions involving my account. At that time, I was informed that nothing could be done until the transactions fully posted to my account. I requested that the payments be stopped immediately, but I was told by XXXX XXXX that they could not stop them. 

Two unauthorized transactions of {$3000.00} each, totaling {$6000.00}, were transferred from my XXXX XXXX account to another Cash App account. The funds were then converted into Bitcoin/virtual currency and sent to wallet addresses that I do not recognize or control. I did not authorize these Bitcoin purchases, cryptocurrency transfers, or wallet transactions.

The transaction records later provided by Cash App show multiple Bitcoin transfers, XXXX XXXX withdrawals, and internal currency exchanges occurring between XX/XX/year> and XX/XX/year>. These transactions were not authorized by me.

I immediately contacted Cash App and opened fraud disputes regarding the unauthorized activity. I have submitted four claims with Cash App, and each claim has been denied. XXXXXXXX XXXX has stated that because the funds were transferred through Cash App, they consider the matter to be in Cash Apps hands.

I submitted an IC3 complaint with the FBI and am requesting a full investigation into the unauthorized transactions, Bitcoin wallet transfers, recipient wallet addresses, and any accounts associated with this activity, along with reimbursement for the stolen funds.

Also, those screenshots/documents are definitely worth attaching to the CFPB complaint since they show : dates, transaction types, Bitcoin transfers, XXXX withdrawals, and the {$3000.00} deposits/transfers.

Summary of Issue : Navy Federal Credit Union denied my fraud claim for an unauthorized ACH withdrawal of {$3700.00} from my account on XX/XX/year>, even though I reported the fraud immediately and provided extensive evidence showing that the transaction was not authorized by me. 

Details of the XXXX : On XX/XX/year>, I received a text message from Navy Federal stating that an external account ending in XXXX had been added to my account. I did not authorize this and received no twofactor authentication request. 
On XX/XX/year>, I discovered that {$3700.00} had been withdrawn via XXXX transfer labeled XXXX Huddles. XXXX XXXX confirmed the funds were sent to a XXXX XXXX account and stated this XXXX be part of a XXXX XXXX scam trend they have been dealing with. 

I filed a police report and an FBI XXXX complaint for interstate wire fraud and identity theft. I also provided Navy Federal with screenshots of threatening messages from the suspect, who sent me a screenshot of my XXXX XXXX profile photo, confirming access to my information. 

New Evidence of Email Compromise : After filing my initial report, I discovered that my XXXX email account ( xxxxxxxxxxxxx ) had been compromised. A hidden rule had been created that automatically moved any email containing the word Navy to the Deleted Items folder. This prevented me from seeing Navy Federal alerts, including the email confirming the {$3700.00} transfer. 
Screenshots show the rule configuration and the deleted Navy Federal emails, proving that someone accessed my email and concealed bank notifications. 

Why XXXX Filing This Complaint : Despite this clear evidence of unauthorized access and fraud, Navy Federal denied my claim without providing a detailed explanation or investigation file. Under the XXXX XXXX Transfer Act ( 15 U.S.C. 1693 et seq. ) and Regulation E ( 12 CFR 1005 ), the credit union is required to conduct a reasonable investigation and reimburse unauthorized transfers unless it can prove authorization. I reported the fraud promptly and provided substantial documentation. 

Requested Resolution : I am requesting that Navy Federal : Reopen and reinvestigate my fraud claim under Regulation XXXX. 

Provide a written explanation of the denial and the evidence used. 

Reimburse the {$3700.00} withdrawn without authorization. 

Review the evidence of email compromise as part of the investigation. 

Attachments Available : Police report and amendment FBI XXXX complaint confirmation Screenshots of Outlook rule and deleted Navy Federal emails Screenshots of fraudulent transaction and threatening messages
