text:
In XX/XX/XXXX, I was the victim of a realestate wire fraud scheme. On XX/XX/XXXX, I received an email that appeared to come from my closing attorney. It included the attorneys letterhead and my correct closing statement, but the wiring instructions were fraudulent. Based on that email, on XX/XX/XXXX I wired {$620000.00} from my XXXX account to a Regions Bank account. 
On XX/XX/XXXX, when my closing attorney informed me the funds had not been received, I immediately filed an FBI IC3 report and went in person to the Regions Bank branch on XXXX XXXX in XXXX, SC to report the fraud. Regions staff took a report but did not provide me a copy. They instructed me to have XXXX initiate a recall. XXXX informed me that Regions required a letter of indemnification, which XXXX would not provide, so no recall was initiated. This was contrary to FBI guidance that both banks should issue letters of indemnification and attempt a recall.

I later learned from investigators that two checks were issued from the recipient account. I was never told the dates or amounts of these withdrawals, so I do not know whether they occurred before or after I reported the fraud. These withdrawals reduced the amount ultimately recoverable. I also learned from the XXXX XXXX Police Department that Regions received a subpoena on XX/XX/XXXX but told law enforcement they were too busy to respond at that time. This delayed the investigation and recovery efforts.

Despite my reporting the fraud on XX/XX/XXXX, I do not know when Regions actually froze the account. During this period, funds may have continued to leave the account. This raises concerns about Regions fraudresponse procedures and whether they acted promptly to protect the remaining funds. 
On XX/XX/XXXX, the FBI informed me that Regions had received a federal seizure notice. In XX/XX/XXXX, the FBI seized {$430000.00} from the account. I filed a petition for remission and recovered that amount on XX/XX/XXXX. However, {$180000.00} of the original wire remains unaccounted for. The FBI advised me to file a FOIA request, but the responses were heavily redacted and did not explain what happened to the missing funds. 
Regions Banks actions and inaction including delays, failure to assist after fraud was reported, failure to respond to law enforcement, and allowing withdrawals that reduced the recoverable amount directly harmed me. I am requesting that the CFPB review Regions Banks handling of this matter and determine whether they acted appropriately under consumerprotection laws.
label: payment_transfer
source_product: Money transfer, virtual currency, or money service

text:
Subject : Unauthorized Western Union Transfers Account Compromise and Failure to Prevent Fraud I am submitting this complaint regarding unauthorized transfers made from my Western Union account after my account was compromised/hacked. 

In XX/XX/year>, four unauthorized money transfers totaling approximately {$79000.00} were sent from my account without my knowledge or authorization : Money Transfer XXXX XXXX {$190.00} Money Transfer XXXX  XXXX {$190.00} Money Transfer XXXX  XXXX {$190.00} Money Transfer XXXX  XXXX {$190.00} I discovered the fraudulent activity almost immediately and reported it to Western Union within approximately 30 minutes of the transactions being made. During that report, Western Union blocked and closed my account due to the fraudulent activity.

Despite receiving immediate notice that the transactions were unauthorized and fraudulent, Western Union failed to stop, freeze, or prevent the transfers from being completed before the funds were credited to the recipient account ( s ).

Western Union later informed me that they could not reverse the transactions because the recipient bank confirmed that the funds had already been credited. However, this response does not address the core issue that : - The transfers were unauthorized.

- My account had been compromised.

- I reported the fraud immediately.

- and Western Union itself considered the activity suspicious enough to block and close my account.

Western Union has also failed to provide : - Details of its fraud investigation.

- Login/IP/device access records.

- Information regarding authentication methods used.

- or an explanation of what actions were taken after I reported the fraud.

I acted responsibly and notified the company as soon as I became aware of the unauthorized activity. I should not be held financially responsible for fraudulent transactions resulting from account compromise and possible failures in fraud prevention procedures.

text:
At approximately XXXX XXXX on XX/XX/year> I went into the Well Fargo branch located at XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, XXXX, Fl XXXX to cash 3 checks and I was XXXX profiled, discriminated against, denied service, verbally attacked, and escorted out. 

Initially, when I presented the checks, the teller informed me that there was a {$10.00} check cashing fee for each check. I thought this was excessive considering the checks were drawn on Wells Fargo Bank and it does not seem right that a bank can charge a fee to 'Pay to the Order of " their own check. 

Because of the fee, I decided to only cash one of the checks. I presented the check and my Florida Driver 's License. 

The teller then asked me for a second form of ID, apparently because my state issued ID was not sufficient, so I produced the second ID. 

The teller scrutinized my IDs, scrutinized the check, and scrutinized the computer and then beckoned to her colleague telling her colleague that she needed an 'override '. 

When the colleague arrived, the teller informed her that everything looked alright. The colleague then scrutinized my IDs, scrutinized the check, and scrutinized her computer and confirmed everything was in order. She then instructed the teller to call the issuer of the check and verify they had actually written it. 

I my life, I have never had a bank or any other check cashing establishment call the issuer of a check to verify they had written the check I was cashing. At this point I felt racially profiled and discriminated against. 

The teller calls the issuer, who confirmed she wrote the check and how much it was for. 

The teller comes back to me and asks me to present my IDs, which she and her colleague had both already scrutinized, again, as if neither did what they were supposed to have done with it before. 

I was being racially profiled and I said so. The Drive-up teller, who heretofore had not been a part of anything, interjects herself by verbally assaulting me. When I told her that I was not speaking to her she raised her voice, so I raised mine. 

The colleague to the original teller to return the check denying me service. I was then escorted out of the bank.

text:
Consumer Complaint Against Wells Fargo Bank I am filing this complaint regarding Wells Fargos handling of a check deposit in the amount of {$4700.00}, which has caused me financial hardship, confusion, and a negative account balance despite the funds reportedly clearing. 

On XX/XX/year>, I deposited a check for {$4700.00} into my Wells Fargo account. Wells Fargo initially verified the deposit and released {$400.00} into my account. I used those funds after being told that the remaining balance would be available the next day. 

On XX/XX/year>, Wells Fargo reversed the {$400.00} that had already been made available, which placed my account into a negative balance. The remaining funds from the check were also not released. When I called Wells Fargo customer care, I was told there was an issue involving the other bank and possible suspicious activity, but Wells Fargo did not provide a clear explanation or transparent information about what was happening. 

I then contacted the bank the check was drawn from, XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, and also spoke with the owner of the account who signed the check during a three-way call. They confirmed that the funds were available in the account. When I called Wells Fargo back, I was told that the processing department had placed a hold on the funds until XX/XX/year>, supposedly to investigate the issue. I explained that this was causing me financial hardship, but Wells Fargo told me that the system placed the hold and that they could not change it. 

Because this occurred over the weekend, I waited until Monday, XX/XX/year>, and went in person to the Wells Fargo branch located at XXXX and XXXX in XXXX XXXX, Nevada. I spoke with a banker, who made calls to try to determine why the hold had been placed on the check. The branch manager then became involved. I was told that the check did not show who it was from, or did not have a company name or logo, and that processing said id have to wait til XX/XX/year>. Even then they stated they probably wouldn't deposit the check. They suggested I contact the owner of the check and have him do a wire. After leaving the Wells Fargo branch I contacted the one who wrote the check to me and had him do a wire. He sends wire receipt. 

On XX/XX/year>, I received a call from the owner who signed the check. He stated that a wire transfer did not go through because the check had already cleared through Wells Fargo on XX/XX/year> and he didn't have the funds available to cover the wire and the check. I then called XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX and spoke with XXXX, a branch manager. She confirmed that the funds from the check had cleared on XX/XX/year>. 

After receiving that confirmation, I returned to the same Wells Fargo branch and spoke with the banker and another manager. They stated that they did not know why the funds had not been released. I was then given a different explanation, including that the check had no company name and also something about another check with the same check number, which did not make sense to me. I was again told that I would have to wait until XX/XX/year>. I asked how I was supposed to buy food or handle basic needs while my money was being withheld, especially after the funds had already cleared. I left the branch to avoid conflict. 

I then contacted the Wells Fargo Executive Office of the President. The first time, I was connected with Risk or the Prevention Contact Center. I was again told that they could not do anything about the hold, that the system placed it, that no one could override it, and that a temporary credit was not an option, even though humans created this system. Meanwhile, my account remained more than {$400.00} negative, and I was at risk of additional fees and financial harm.

I specifically asked where my money was being held and why Wells Fargo was continuing to withhold the funds after XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX confirmed the check had cleared and I had a physical screenshot receipt of it clearing on XX/XX/XXXX along with branch managers confirmation. I was told that Wells Fargo wanted to hold the funds in case the other bank stopped payment. However, I have been given multiple contradictory explanations by Wells Fargo, including alleged suspicious activity, an issue with the check format, a missing company name or logo, and another check with the same check number that nothing Wells Fargo has said makes sense.

I contacted the Wells Fargo Executive Office of the President again and explained that Risk had not resolved the issue and that the funds had already cleared on XX/XX/year>. I was told the matter would be escalated and that someone would call me back the same day. I did not receive a call back. Instead, I received an email stating that Wells Fargo would look into the matter case # XXXX, but that it could take up to 10 days. That response is not sufficient given the financial hardship caused by Wells Fargos actions.

The facts are as follows : Wells Fargo initially verified the check and released {$400.00} to me. After I used those funds, Wells Fargo clawed the money back the next day and placed my account into a negative balance. Wells Fargo then continued withholding the remaining deposit and provided inconsistent explanations for the hold. XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX confirmed that the funds were available and later confirmed that the check cleared with Wells Fargo XX/XX/year>. The check was professionally created by XXXX XXXX which banks are very familiar with. Despite this, Wells Fargo has continued to withhold the funds, while my account remains negative and subject to overdraft fees.
label: payment_transfer

Shortly after the theft, an unknown individual accessed my Chime mobile application and initiated multiple transfers to other Chime accounts * * without my authorization * *. I * * reported the fraud immediately * * and submitted all requested documentation, including the police report. However, Chime denied my claim stating that no error occurred because the transfers were initiated from a device with my account information saved. 

Under the * * Electronic Fund Transfer Act and Regulation E * *, a transaction initiated by a third party using a * * stolen device or compromised credentials is not considered an authorized electronic fund transfer * *. A financial institution must conduct a * * reasonable investigation * * before denying such a claim. 

Chimes denial appears to rely solely on the fact that the transactions originated from a device associated with my account rather than evidence that * * I personally authorized the transfers * *. This does not satisfy the investigation standards required under Regulation E. 

I am requesting that Chime and its partner bank * * reopen the investigation * *, review the receiving Chime accounts identified in the police report, and provide the documentation relied upon to deny my claim. 

I respectfully request reimbursement of the * * {$3900.00} in unauthorized transfers * * and confirmation that the investigation has been conducted in compliance with Regulation E.

text:
On XX/XX/year>, I received a call from XXXX. My caller ID displayed " Chase, '' which led me to trust the caller. The individual claimed to be a Chase representative and stated that my account had been compromised via XXXX, alleging that a person named XXXX XXXX had attempted XXXX unauthorized withdrawals : {$990.00} and {$490.00}. 

The caller identified himself as XXXX, with Chase employee ID : XXXX. He immediately provided a case number XXXX along with XXXX " cancellation numbers '' : XXXX and XXXX, which I later realized correspond directly to the XXXX transaction amounts, a detail that clearly demonstrates this scheme was pre-planned and targeted. 

The caller then instructed me to log into my Chase account and add XXXX XXXX recipients, claiming they were Chase bank managers who would process the cancellations : Recipient XXXX : XXXX XXXX XXXX Phone : ( XXXX ) XXXX Amount : {$990.00} Recipient XXXX : XXXX XXXX XXXX : ( XXXX ) XXXX Amount : {$490.00} At some point during the call, the caller asked me what the maximum allotted XXXX transfer limit displayed on my account was. Since I was navigating a different page of my account portal at the time, I was unable to locate the information he was requesting. 

For the first transaction, the caller assured me the funds would not actually be sent and initially it appeared that way. However, after completing the second transaction to XXXX XXXX, the {$490.00} transferred successfully. At that moment, I realized this was a scam call and that the caller was conducting a qualifying impersonation scam. 

The caller went silent but did not hang up I could still hear background noise consistent with a call center environment. Upon refreshing my account, I confirmed that both XXXX transfers had been processed, totaling {$1400.00}. I immediately contacted Chase Bank and reported the incident. 

Chase reference number : XXXX. 

I also filed a report with the XXXX XXXX Police Department. Police report number : XXXX XXXX. The responding XXXX, XXXX XXXX, XXXX # XXXX, informed me that the department has recently received multiple complaints from other XXXX XXXX residents describing the same scheme. 

I am reporting this as a wire fraud and bank impersonation scheme, potentially violating 18 U.S.C. 1343 ( wire fraud ) and 18 U.S.C. 1344 ( bank fraud ). 

These scammers were highly sophisticated and impersonated Chase employees with convincing accuracy. They spoofed Chase 's caller ID, used authentic banking terminology, and guided me through each step exactly as a legitimate Chase representative would. The spoofed caller ID was particularly disarming and created a false sense of security. text:
I am writing to formally appeal your denial of my fraud claim regarding unauthorized transactions totaling over {$2400.00} from my accounts. 
The disputed activity consists of an unauthorized transfer of funds from my savings account into my checking account, followed by three withdrawals. These transactions occurred on XX/XX/year>, and I did not authorize, initiate, or have any knowledge of this activity. 
Your denial cites that the card was in my possession at the time of the disputed activity and that there were no further card-present transactions after the card was closed. I respectfully dispute this conclusion. 
Under Regulation E of the Electronic Fund Transfer Act, unauthorized electronic fund transfers are defined as transfers initiated without the consumers authority and from which the consumer receives no benefit. I meet this definition. Possession of my debit card does not constitute proof that I authorized these transactions, nor does it eliminate the possibility of electronic fraud, account compromise, or unauthorized access through other means.

Additionally, the transaction pattern, an internal transfer immediately followed by rapid withdrawals, is consistent with known fraud schemes and does not reflect my normal account activity. If this matter is not resolved appropriately, I am prepared to escalate this dispute to pursue recovery through legal means if necessary.

text:
This is the second unauthorized transaction on my Wells Fargo joint personal checking account in XXXX weeks. Both incidents were initiated through my joint account holders online banking login without his knowledge or authorization. 
On XX/XX/year>, an unauthorized transfer was made from my business checking account ( where my joint account holder is a co-signer ) into our joint personal checking account. I reported it to Wells Fargo and filed a fraud claim ( Claim No. XXXX ). The bank denied the claim, stating it was made by someone with legitimate access. I was able to transfer the funds back myself. As remediation, Wells Fargo required me to change my account numbers and online banking credentials. The bank did not disclose to me whose login was used to initiate the XX/XX/XXXX transaction. I now believe the remediation they applied may have been irrelevant to the actual breach. 
On XX/XX/year>, at approximately XXXXXXXX XXXX XXXX, someone accessed the account online and changed the phone number from my legitimate number to XXXX ( Maryland area code ). This redirected verification codes and disabled text alerts. At XXXXXXXX XXXX text alerts were canceled for the new number. At XXXX XXXX, the fraudulent number was removed. Between the phone number change and its removal, an unauthorized wire transfer of {$17000.00} was initiated again through my joint account holders online login. 
I called Wells Fargo within XXXX minutes of receiving the phone number change notification. I was placed on hold for approximately XXXX minutes and then transferred multiple times before I was able to share critical information about the unauthorized transaction with anyone who could act on it. This wasted precious time during which the wire could potentially have been stopped. 
The wire was sent to XXXX XXXX at XXXX XXXX XXXX in XXXX, XXXX. Fedwire number XXXX. Confirmation number XXXX. The wire has been completed. I do not know the beneficiary. The purpose was listed as Family or friend. 
I visited a Wells Fargo branch in person. The following failures occurred : 1. The banks fraud department told me they could not identify the transaction type, beneficiary, or receiving bank because the transaction was pending. My joint account holder subsequently found the completed wire with full details visible in the account. The banks own system showed the transaction had already been completed while they were telling me it was pending.

2. Despite the transaction showing as pending, Wells Fargo stated they could not stop or cancel it.

3. I received contradictory explanations for how the unauthorized activity occurred. XXXX fraud representatives stated the wire was not done online. A branch representative stated the phone number change was done online. A fraud supervisor then stated it may have been done over the phone. Wells Fargo ultimately confirmed the wire was initiated online through my joint account holders login. 
4. The call with the fraud supervisor was disconnected before my questions could be answered.

5. The fraud investigator focused on questions about my account access rather than on stopping the unauthorized transaction. 
6. When Wells Fargo denied my XX/XX/XXXX claim citing legitimate access, they did not explain whose access they were referring to or disclose which login was used. Had they provided this information, I could have taken steps to secure the compromised credentials and the XX/XX/XXXX wire may not have occurred. 
I have filed XXXX reports with the FBIs IC3 including the Fedwire number. I have also notified XXXX XXXX XXXX fraud department. 
My concerns are : ( 1 ) Wells Fargo denied my first claim without providing enough information for me to understand and address the actual vulnerability ; ( 2 ) the banks remediation after the first incident did not address the actual compromised credentials ; ( 3 ) the bank allowed the account phone number to be changed without adequate verification ; ( 4 ) the bank told me the transaction was pending and unidentifiable when it had already been completed with full details in their system ; ( 5 ) the bank could not stop a transaction they claimed had not yet settled ; and ( 6 ) when I called within minutes of the unauthorized activity, hold times and repeated transfers delayed the banks response during a critical window.

text:
I am filing this complaint regarding Cash Apps failure to properly investigate and resolve unauthorized electronic fund transfers and their violation of Regulation E requirements. 

On XX/XX/year>, unauthorized withdrawals totaling thousands of dollars were made from my account after my phone was stolen. I did not authorize these transactions or grant access to any individual. 

I initially filed a dispute, which Cash App denied on XX/XX/year> under XXXX XXXX. XXXX. 

I then filed a separate and independent dispute on XX/XX/year> ( XXXX XXXX. XXXX ) regarding these unauthorized transactions. 

This XX/XX/year> dispute has not been properly handled. 

To date : - Cash App has not issued any written determination for XXXX XXXX. XXXX - More than 10 business days have passed since the dispute was filed - Cash App has not issued provisional credit as required under Regulation E - Cash App has instead improperly relied on the prior denial ( Case No. XXXX ), which is a separate case Recently, Cash App provided documentation they claim supports their denial. However, the documentation only consists of a basic transaction summary listing dates, amounts, and Manual Cash Out entries.

This documentation does not demonstrate authorization and does not reflect a reasonable investigation. 

Cash App failed to provide : - Device or session data tied to the transactions - IP address or location information - Authentication records ( PIN, biometric, or verification steps ) - Any evidence that I initiated or authorized these transactions Providing a list of transactions only confirms that the transactions occurred. It does not prove that I authorized them.

Under the Electronic Fund Transfer Act ( 15 U.S.C. 1693 ) and Regulation E ( 12 C.F.R. 1005.11 ), Cash App is required to conduct a reasonable investigation and either complete it within 10 business days or issue provisional credit.

Cash App has failed to meet both requirements. 

Additionally, their response using the wrong case number demonstrates that they have not properly investigated the XX/XX/year> dispute. 

This matter has also been escalated to arbitration due to Cash Apps continued failure to comply with federal law.

text: On XX/XX/XXXX, I made a large deposit of {$1000.00}. I know it had XXXX {$100.00} bills, and the rest was a mix of {$20.00}, {$10.00}, {$5.00} and mainly {$1.00} bills. So, I straightened that bill out and added it to my stack of singles I had left and went to deposit it. At first, the deposit thing went to close on top of the stack, and I didn't have them positioned good, so I took the money out because I didnt want it to cause an issue. I then hit deposit more and this time successfully put the stack in the machine. At this point, I hear the ATM sound like it's processing my second deposit. Since there were other people behind me, I didn't want to take longer than needed so I drove around to park and checked my bank account to see if it all processed correctly. I saw my bank account only reflected the {$960.00} deposit and not the full {$1000.00}. I went inside the branch, and I explained what happened to an employee and they told me that I had to call the number on the back of my debit card. The second I got home ; I made that call and filed a claim and explained everything that happened to the claims department. They gave me temporary credit for the missing {$56.00} on XX/XX/XXXX while they did their research. text:
This is the most important field in the CFPB form. Important guidance first : Note the warning : " Do not include personal information, such as your name, account number, address, Social Security number, etc. '' So this version needs to be different again no personal identifiers. 
About the " publish on consumerfinance.gov '' checkbox : This is a personal choice. Here 's the tradeoff : Check it : Your complaint becomes public on CFPB 's database ( with personal info redacted ). This adds public pressure on Coinbase and helps other potential victims of similar issues find your case. Some plaintiffs ' attorneys actively monitor these databases looking for class-action patterns. 
Don't check it : Your complaint is still investigated and Coinbase still has to respond it just isn't public. 

I'd recommend checking it. The CFPB redacts personally identifying details, and a public record of Coinbase failing to flag a clear account-takeover pattern can help others ( and potentially benefit you if a class action ever materializes ). But it's your call. 
Paste this into the description box : The CFPB framing is different from IC3 and FTC here, the focus is on Coinbase 's conduct as a regulated financial company, not just describing the theft. So the narrative emphasizes what Coinbase failed to do. 

On XX/XX/year> at XXXX XXXX, an unauthorized change was made to my Coinbase account 's XXXX verification settings. I did not initiate this change. I retained the email Coinbase sent confirming the XXXX change. 
Approximately XXXX hours later, on XX/XX/year> at XXXX XXXX, an unauthorized withdrawal of XXXX XXXX ( approximately {$9100.00} USD ) was executed from my Coinbase account and sent to an external XXXX  address. I did not authorize this transaction. I was traveling out of state at the time, attending a family wedding. 
The XXXX gap between the unauthorized XXXX change and the unauthorized withdrawal corresponds to Coinbase 's XXXX XXXX withdrawal hold period. This pattern - XXXX change followed by full account drain at the moment the security hold expires- is a textbook indicator of account takeover. Coinbase 's automated fraud detection systems should reasonably be expected to flag this pattern, particularly when the withdrawal occurs at XXXX XXXX  and constitutes the entire account balance. To my knowledge, Coinbase did not pause, hold, or seek verification on this withdrawal despite these red flags. 
Aside from the XXXX email, I did not receive any of the standard Coinbase notifications that should have accompanied this activity ( new device sign-in alert, withdrawal authorization request, withdrawal confirmation email ). This indicates either that the attacker was able to modify notification settings on my account without further security checks, or that Coinbase failed to send these standard alerts. 
I contacted Coinbase to report the unauthorized activity and request investigation, recovery, and a refund. Coinbase declined to refund the funds and, to my knowledge, did not conduct or share with me a detailed investigation of how the perpetrator gained access, what IP addresses were used, or what device fingerprints were associated with the unauthorized activity. I have not received any meaningful information from Coinbase about the security failure that allowed this theft. 
I have since traced the stolen XXXX across the public blockchain through multiple intermediary " burner '' wallets to what appears to be a custodial cryptocurrency service. I have filed reports with the FBI 's IC3, the FTC, and the XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX ( Report XXXX ). 
WHAT I AM REQUESTING FROM CFPB : That Coinbase be required to provide a complete copy of the account access logs, login history, IP addresses, device fingerprints, and account-change records for my account covering XXXX XXXX XXXX 
That Coinbase explain why their fraud detection did not flag a XXXX change followed by a full account drain occurring at XXXX XXXX  as a high-risk pattern requiring additional verification. 
That Coinbase explain why standard withdrawal notifications were not received. 
That Coinbase be required to participate in good-faith investigation alongside law enforcement to identify the perpetrator, given that they hold records that I as a customer can not access. 
Consideration of refund or partial refund given the security failures involved.
label: payment_transfer
source_product: Money transfer, virtual currency, or money service

text:
I am filing a complaint regarding PayPals handling of a chargeback dispute and appeal involving Case ID XXXX in the amount of {$1800.00}. 

The underlying issue involved a returned item that was not delivered to my verified business address. USPS tracking documentation confirmed the package was delivered to XXXX XXXX XXXX, while my verified business and return address associated with the transaction is in XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX USPS documentation also confirmed the package was left with an unknown individual not associated with my business.

I submitted organized evidence to PayPal during the appeal process, including : * USPS tracking records * Delivery confirmation documentation * Invoice records showing the verified return address * Communication records showing the buyer acknowledged sending the item to a different address * Documentation showing no alternate return address was authorized Initially, PayPal representatives provided conflicting instructions regarding how to submit an appeal. I was instructed to send documentation to an email address that later generated an automated response stating the address was no longer monitored. After multiple calls, another representative manually uploaded and escalated my appeal documents to PayPals backend review team.

After waiting beyond the stated review timeframe, I contacted PayPal again and received inconsistent and conflicting information from representatives regarding whether my appeal existed, whether it was being reviewed, and whether the issue involved Seller Protection or a chargeback appeal review. 

I also requested clarification regarding : * the basis for the denial, * whether my submitted evidence had been reviewed, * and escalation to a supervisor. 

PayPal did not provide a meaningful explanation addressing the submitted evidence. Instead, I later received a generic denial stating only that the buyers processor upheld the chargeback decision.

At no point did PayPal explain why the documented evidence showing delivery to the wrong city and wrong recipient was rejected or disregarded.

My complaint is not simply that the chargeback was denied, but that PayPal failed to provide a clear, consistent, and meaningful review process for the submitted appeal evidence and failed to adequately address the documented facts provided during the appeal.
label: payment_transfer
